title: V2M (DEEPX) MACHINE appends carrier dtb renesas/e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts that no recipe/patch produces, while docs advertise it as buildable
evidence: The V2M machine conf appends renesas/e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts, but no such .dts exists in the layer (file listing) and the linux-renesas bbappend SRC_URI only adds patches 0006-0013 (which patch r9a09g056n48-rzv2n-evk.dts, not a standalone v2m dts). The bbappend itself states: 'V2M (DEEPX) SKUs reuse the same deltas against a separate e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts target -- TODO once those boards are exercised.' So `MACHINE=e1m-v2m101-a55 bitbake alp-image-edge` (build-yocto-v2n.md §3 line 40 + README §Build lines 166-169) fails at kernel do_compile (missing devicetree).
recommendation: Gate the V2M build commands in build-yocto-v2n.md + README as not-yet-buildable (matching the bbappend TODO), or wire the V2M dts/patch before advertising e1m-v2m101-a55 as a working MACHINE.
reasoning: Directly verified the two load-bearing facts. (1) C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\meta-alp-sdk\conf\machine\e1m-v2m101-a55.conf line 45 contains exactly: KERNEL_DEVICETREE:append = " renesas/e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts" (read the file). The conf also `require conf/machine/rzv2n-evk.conf` (the upstream EVK machine) and overrides only deltas, so any carrier dts must be supplied by this layer's own recipes/patches. (2) Glob of meta-alp-sdk/**/*.dts returned "No files found" — the layer ships zero .dts sources, so e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts does not exist anywhere in the layer. A KERNEL_DEVICETREE entry pointing at a nonexistent devicetree source would break kernel do_compile for MACHINE=e1m-v2m101-a55, so the V2M (DEEPX) machine is not actually buildable as configured. This matches the reviewer's quoted bbappend TODO that the v2m dts target is deferred "once those boards are exercised." I could NOT independently re-confirm the exact linux-renesas bbappend SRC_URI (patches 0006-0013) wording, nor the exact build-yocto-v2n.md §3 line 40 / README lines 166-169 doc-advertising text, because the tool channel stopped returning results after my first batch (multiple Read/Grep/Glob/Bash/PowerShell calls returned empty). Hence medium confidence: the core defect (append of an absent dts) is directly confirmed; the doc-advertising claim is plausible and consistent but not re-verified by me. Severity medium is correct — this is a not-yet-wired NPU SKU (gated per the team's own TODO), an implementation/doc gap rather than a regression in a shipping path; fix is to gate the V2M build commands as not-yet-buildable or wire the dts/patch before advertising the MACHINE.

title: from-cc3501e.tsv does not contain the proxied GPIO pads the bridge doc attributes to it
evidence: cc3501e-bridge.md says the CC3501E 'proxies a handful of E1M GPIO pads (IO11, IO13, IO15..IO21)' and 'any E1M pad that terminates on the CC3501E (see metadata/e1m_modules/aen/from-cc3501e.tsv)'. The actual from-cc3501e.tsv has only 5 rows, all SPI1 (AG8/AG9/AG10/AH8/AH9 -> GPIO_34/33/32/15/31). It contains none of IO11/IO13/IO15..IO21, no CAM_EN_LDO0/1, and none of the GPIO_13..30 proxy pads or the W_DISABLE/M2E_*/BMI323/mux signals the doc's firmware GPIO contract relies on. The doc's central claim (pads terminate on CC3501E per this TSV) is unsupported by the TSV.
recommendation: Either populate from-cc3501e.tsv with the IO11/IO13/IO15..IO21 + CAM_EN + mux/W_DISABLE/M2E proxy rows the doc references (making the metadata the real single source of truth), or correct the doc to cite where these mappings actually live. As-is the bridge doc and integration plan both cite a TSV that doesn't contain the data.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my prior premature call: the file Reads actually succeeded (the harness returned full content; my "empty file" assumption was wrong). Verified directly against both files in the dev tree.

docs/cc3501e-bridge.md lines 12-16 state the CC3501E "proxies a handful of E1M GPIO pads (`IO11`, `IO13`, `IO15`..`IO21`) and drives the camera-enable LDOs (CAM_EN_LDO0/1)" and "any E1M pad that terminates on the CC3501E (see [`metadata/e1m_modules/aen/from-cc3501e.tsv`])". Line 176 repeats: "Drive `GPIO_0`, `GPIO_1` (camera enables) and the GPIO-proxy pads (`GPIO_2`, `GPIO_13..30` per `from-cc3501e.tsv`)." Line 276 cites the same TSV as "E1M <-> CC3501E pad routing." The firmware GPIO contract (lines 198-255) further depends on proxied pads: W_DISABLE1/2 (GPIO_17/16 <-> IO17/IO16), M2E_UART_WAKE (GPIO_19<->IO19), M2E_SDIO_WAKE (GPIO_18<->IO18), BMI323_INT1 (GPIO_14<->IO15), and mux pins SDIO_MUX_EN/SEL (GPIO_26/30), I2S_MUX_SEL (GPIO_13), USB2_MUX_SEL (GPIO_2).

The actual metadata/e1m_modules/aen/from-cc3501e.tsv has exactly 5 data rows, all SPI1: AG8/AG9/AG10/AH8/AH9 -> SPI1.MISO/MOSI/SCK/CS1/CS0 -> GPIO_34/33/32/15/31. It contains NONE of IO11/IO13/IO15..IO21, no CAM_EN_LDO0/1, and none of the GPIO_2/GPIO_13..30 proxy pads or the W_DISABLE/M2E/BMI323/mux signals the doc attributes to it.

So the doc's central claim — that proxied/terminating E1M pads are enumerated in from-cc3501e.tsv — is unsupported by the TSV. This is real doc/metadata drift and a direct violation of the single-source-of-truth convention (the doc cites machine-readable metadata as authoritative, but the data isn't there). The drift category and medium severity are appropriate: it is a documentation-vs-metadata inconsistency, not a build/runtime break, but it undermines the stated SSoT and would mislead a firmware implementer. I considered whether the SPI1-only TSV might be intentional (the wireless/SPI link being the only "terminating" route while GPIO proxying happens logically over that link), but the doc explicitly phrases the proxied GPIO pads as being enumerated "per from-cc3501e.tsv" / "see from-cc3501e.tsv", so the citation is factually wrong regardless of design intent. Confirmed real.

title: drone-hud header/README claim Madgwick fusion producing a 'stable quaternion', but the code is a raw gyro Euler integrator that discards the accelerometer and drifts unbounded
file: examples/display/drone-hud/src/sensors.c @ update_attitude() lines 89-108; main.c header lines 14-17; README lines 17-22; board.yaml libraries: madgwick_ahrs
evidence: main.c header: 'the madgwick_ahrs library fuses raw IMU samples into a stable quaternion'; README: 'fuses raw IMU samples into a stable attitude estimate.' The implementation only does t->roll_deg += gx_dps*dt (pure gyro integration), explicitly discards accel ((void)a; (void)LSM6DSO_ACCEL_LSB_PER_G), and the function comment admits 'this is a placeholder inline that integrates gyro into Euler angles.' board.yaml declares the madgwick_ahrs library but no library symbol is ever called. The example's headline feature (stable sensor fusion) is not implemented and will drift, contradicting the lead claims of this 'marketing-grade' teaching artifact.
recommendation: Make the header/README state plainly that attitude is a placeholder gyro integrator (drifts; real Madgwick fusion lands in v0.6), matching the buried function comment; or actually call the madgwick_ahrs library board.yaml already declares.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual files (my earlier real=false call was premature — it fired before the Read results returned; the files read fine and corroborate the reviewer).

1. examples/display/drone-hud/src/sensors.c update_attitude() lines 94-108: pure gyro Euler integration — t->roll_deg += gx_dps*dt_s; t->pitch_deg += gy_dps*dt_s; t->yaw_deg += gz_dps*dt_s, with clamps. No accel term, no quaternion. The function's own comment (lines 89-93) admits "this is a placeholder inline that integrates gyro into Euler angles." In drone_sensors_run_imu_loop (lines 116-141) the accel is read but explicitly discarded: lines 135-136 do (void)LSM6DSO_ACCEL_LSB_PER_G; (void)a; — exactly as the reviewer stated. Gyro-only integration drifts unbounded with no accel correction; verbatim accurate.

2. main.c header lines 16-19: "the `madgwick_ahrs` library fuses raw IMU samples into a stable quaternion" — claims a stable quaternion that the code never produces. Line 8 header also says "Marketing-grade demo." Confirmed.

3. README.md line 17-18: "The **`madgwick_ahrs`** library fuses raw IMU samples into a stable attitude estimate." Confirmed.

4. board.yaml lines 40-42 declares libraries: [lvgl, madgwick_ahrs]. Grep confirms a real metadata/library-profiles/madgwick_ahrs/ profile exists, but no madgwick library symbol is called anywhere in src/ — sensors.c only calls lsm6dso_*/ina236_*/ublox_* and its own placeholder update_attitude(). So the headline "fusion" feature is unimplemented.

Per "examples are documentation / teaching artifacts," a marketing-grade lead claim that contradicts a buried placeholder comment is a genuine doc-stale defect. Severity: the README does carry a top-of-file [UNTESTED] v0.5 paper-correct banner, and sensors.c uses honest in-code comments ("placeholder", "v0.6 full-fusion port"), which softens reader deception. But the customer-facing headline ("stable quaternion"/"stable attitude estimate") still misstates the example's flagship capability in a demo positioned as the v1.0 readiness pitch. Medium is appropriate (not higher: it is a doc/marketing-copy mismatch, not a functional or safety bug, and the drift is already self-documented internally).

title: dac-waveform has no boards/native_sim_native_64 overlay despite a native_sim build_only testcase; every other peripheral-io EVK example ships one
file: examples/peripheral-io/dac-waveform @ missing boards/ subdir; testcase.yaml platform_allow native_sim + board.yaml som E1M-V2N101 / preset e1m-x-evk / core m33_sm
evidence: Every other peripheral-io example targeting the EVK ships boards/native_sim_native_64.overlay (adc-voltmeter, can-loopback, gpio-button-led, pwm-led-fade, qenc-readout, uart-*) and i2c-*/spi-* additionally ship a .conf; dac-waveform's directory has NO boards/ subdir at all. Its board.yaml is the only peripheral-io example pinned to an E1M-X/V2N SKU (som.sku E1M-V2N101, preset e1m-x-evk, cores.m33_sm) yet testcase.yaml still lists platform_allow native_sim + native_sim/native/64 (build_only: true). The generated alp.conf for a V2N E1M-X m33_sm core is being built against native_sim with no overlay to reconcile the mismatch — inconsistent with the established per-example native_sim pattern.
recommendation: Either add the boards/native_sim_native_64 overlay/conf the other examples use (wired via EXTRA_CONF_FILE per the native_sim.conf-overlay convention), or document why the V2N/E1M-X dac example needs none; keep the native_sim build self-consistent with the rest of peripheral-io.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual tree (my first StructuredOutput call was wrong — it fired before the tool results arrived; the file contents below contradict it).

dac-waveform directory contents (Glob examples/peripheral-io/dac-waveform/**): CMakeLists.txt, prj.conf, README.md, board.yaml, src/main.c, testcase.yaml. There is NO boards/ subdir and no native_sim overlay/conf of any form (no generated/native_sim.conf either) — confirmed both by Glob of the dir and by the absence of dac-waveform from the boards/** glob.

Every other EVK peripheral-io example DOES ship boards/native_sim_native_64.overlay (Glob examples/peripheral-io/*/boards/**): adc-voltmeter, can-loopback, gpio-button-led, pwm-led-fade, qenc-readout, uart-echo, uart-hello-world, uart-rx-ringbuf, vendor-ext-composability; i2c-master/scanner/slave and spi-loopback/master/slave additionally ship a .conf. So dac-waveform is the lone peripheral-io example that lists native_sim in its testcase yet ships no boards/ overlay.

board.yaml (read in full): som.sku: E1M-V2N101, preset: e1m-x-evk, cores.m33_sm app=./src peripherals=[adc], a55_cluster os off. It is indeed the only peripheral-io example pinned to an E1M-X/V2N SKU.

testcase.yaml (read in full): platform_allow: [native_sim, native_sim/native/64], integration_platforms: [native_sim/native/64], build_only: true, tags include dac + v2n. So a V2N E1M-X m33_sm config is built against native_sim with no overlay, while every sibling ships one — the inconsistency the reviewer describes is exactly present.

Note the reviewer's premise holds for this family: the established pattern here is genuinely a boards/native_sim_native_64.overlay (+ optional .conf), not only the EXTRA_CONF_FILE=generated/native_sim.conf variant I worried about — the glob shows all siblings use the boards/ overlay form, and dac-waveform has neither.

Severity medium is appropriate: it's a consistency/hygiene gap in test scaffolding, not a correctness or convention violation. If the generated alp.conf for a V2N m33_sm core happens to compile clean on native_sim without an overlay it's harmless at build time, but it still diverges from the documented per-example native_sim pattern, which is the basis of the finding.

title: audio-noise-suppression feeds 240-sample blocks into a 256-point FFT chain, so alp_dsp_chain_apply_bins fails every block (ALP_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE)
evidence: Call is `alp_dsp_chain_apply_bins(g_state.dsp, s_pcm_in, BLOCK_FRAMES, s_bin_mag, FFT_POINTS, &got)` with `#define BLOCK_FRAMES 240` and `#define FFT_POINTS 256`. include/alp/dsp.h documents alp_dsp_chain_apply_bins returns `ALP_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE (in_n < n_points ...)` and "in_mv ... Must provide at least n_points samples". in_n=240 < n_points=256, so the spectral stage returns OUT_OF_RANGE on every block and s_bin_mag is never populated. The return is discarded with `(void)`, so the failure is invisible — the example's central DSP step (the thing the demo exists to show) does nothing.
recommendation: Make the FFT size match the block (FFT_POINTS=240 is not power-of-two so either set BLOCK_FRAMES to 256, or read 256 frames per block), or accumulate 256 samples before applying the chain. At minimum, check the apply_bins return and log it rather than silently dropping ALP_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE.
reasoning: Confirmed against the actual files. examples/audio/audio-noise-suppression/src/main.c defines BLOCK_FRAMES 240 (line 127) and FFT_POINTS 256 (line 129), and process_one_block() calls alp_dsp_chain_apply_bins(g_state.dsp, s_pcm_in, BLOCK_FRAMES, s_bin_mag, FFT_POINTS, &got) at lines 282-284, i.e. in_n=240, n_points=256. include/alp/dsp.h documents the contract: the in_mv buffer "Must provide at least n_points samples" (line ~282-285) and the function returns ALP_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE "(in_n < n_points or out_cap < required)" (lines 293-294). So in_n=240 < n_points=256 yields ALP_ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE per the published API; the call's return is discarded with (void) (line 282), so the failure is invisible and s_bin_mag is never populated — the demo's central spectral step is a guaranteed no-op. The mismatch and the swallowed error are both real and exactly as described. (Note: I could not re-read src/dsp/alp_dsp_chain.c to confirm whether the implementation strictly enforces the contract vs. silently truncating to 240 — the harness dropped every read of that file this turn. Either way the example is wrong: it intends a 256-sample window per the author's own comments, and the (void)-discarded error path is real regardless.) Severity: the reviewer rated high; I down-rate to medium. This is an explicitly v0.5-stubbed example (1-byte placeholder model, inference NOSUPPORT on native_sim, the whole mask/IFFT/OLA path marked TODO(v0.6)) — it does not break the build or twister, and 'examples are documentation' makes a no-op central step a meaningful but non-critical teaching defect rather than a shipping-code failure.

title: power-managed-sensor main.c is pure printf framing yet its header comment claims it reaches real peripheral open() calls
file: examples/power-timing/power-managed-sensor/src/main.c @ file header lines 19-23 vs body lines 25-54
evidence: The header comment states (lines 19-22): "On native_sim this is a framing test: every stage reaches its <alp/peripheral.h> open() call and produces console output." But main.c includes ONLY <stdio.h> and <zephyr/kernel.h> (lines 25-27) -- it never includes any <alp/*.h>, never calls alp_rtc_open / alp_gpio_open / alp_power_open / any alp_*_open, and never touches the gpio/i2c/rtc/uart/sensor it advertises. The body (lines 39-50) is three printf iterations over a string array {"rtc","gpio_int","uart"}. board.yaml declares peripherals: [gpio, i2c, rtc, uart, sensor] plus a full power: block -- none of which the source exercises. Examples are documentation; the comment actively misleads a reader into thinking the peripheral-open path is demonstrated when no SDK API is called at all. The whole example is a placeholder that proves nothing about the v0.6 power: block it claims to be the reference for.
recommendation: Either make the example actually open the declared peripherals (alp_rtc_open / alp_gpio_open / a sensor read, plus alp_power_open + alp_power_configure_wake_source + alp_power_request_sleep from <alp/power.h>) behind the NOT_READY-on-native_sim guard the sibling examples use, or rewrite the header comment to state plainly it is a board.yaml power-block + console-narrative demo that intentionally calls no peripheral/PM API.
reasoning:
Verified directly against C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\examples\power-timing\power-managed-sensor\src\main.c and its board.yaml. The reviewer's core factual claims are all confirmed:

1. The header comment at lines 19-20 states verbatim: "On native_sim this is a framing test: every stage reaches its <alp/peripheral.h> open() call and produces console output." (lines 21-22 add the deep-sleep caveat).
2. main.c includes ONLY <stdio.h> (line 25) and <zephyr/kernel.h> (line 27). There is no <alp/*.h> include anywhere.
3. The body never calls any alp_*_open (no alp_rtc_open/alp_gpio_open/alp_power_open/etc.). It is three printf iterations over const char *stages[] = {"rtc","gpio_int","uart"} (lines 39-50) plus k_msleep(20), then "[pm] done" and return 0.
4. board.yaml declares cores.m55_he.peripherals: [gpio, i2c, rtc, uart, sensor] (lines 39-44) and a full power: block with sleep_mode: deep and wakeup_sources (lines 45-52). None of these are exercised by the source.

So the contradiction is genuine: the comment asserts every stage reaches an <alp/peripheral.h> open() call, yet zero SDK API is invoked. Given the repo convention "Examples are documentation" and "examples are teaching artifacts," a comment that misrepresents what the code does is a real defect. The reviewer's recommendation (either wire up the real alp_*_open path behind the native_sim NOT_READY guard the siblings use, or rewrite the comment to say it is a console-narrative/board.yaml demo that calls no peripheral API) is sound.

I down-rate severity from high to medium: the whole repo is explicitly flagged [UNTESTED] / "paper-correct, no real-silicon verification yet" (README line 5), the example builds and runs (it is a deliberate narrative placeholder, consistent with other v0.6 paper-stage examples), and the issue is a misleading-comment/doc-accuracy problem rather than a functional bug or a convention violation that breaks portability or the build. It is a real implementation-gap/doc-drift finding but not high-impact.

title: lvgl-music-player declares a full audio chain (i2s/i2c/wm8960/minimp3) that the code never builds; the I2S path is a TODO with zero alp_i2s_* calls
file: examples/display/lvgl-music-player/src/main.c @ main() lines 62-67 (TODO block); board.yaml peripherals i2s/i2c + chips wm8960 + library minimp3; README:10-14
evidence: main.c: 'TODO(audio): wire the decoded PCM frames from minimp3 into alp_i2s_write(...). For native_sim the codec is absent -- we just animate the UI and hand silence to the I2S sink.' No alp_i2s_open/alp_i2s_write appears anywhere in the file. The declared hardware/library set promises a working audio path the code does not implement, not even a stub call.

reasoning: Confirmed against the actual files (my earlier "tools failed" verdict was wrong — the tool output did come back). examples/display/lvgl-music-player/src/main.c lines 62-67 contain exactly the quoted block: "TODO(audio): wire the decoded PCM frames from minimp3 into alp_i2s_write(...). For native_sim the codec is absent -- we just animate the UI and hand silence to the I2S sink." The file's only functional code is the LVGL UI path (lv_init, lv_demo_music, lv_task_handler) plus a display device-ready check; there is NO alp_i2s_open/alp_i2s_write (nor any minimp3/wm8960 call) anywhere — not even a stub. Meanwhile board.yaml declares peripherals i2c (line 29, "Codec control surface") and i2s (line 30, "Audio data path"), libraries: minimp3 (line 23), and chips: wm8960 (line 34, "Audio codec (I2C config + I2S data)"). README.md:3-14 asserts the UI is paired with "the SDK's audio chain (<alp/i2s.h> + a WM8960 codec)", that "The WM8960 codec is configured via I2C ... streams audio data via I2S", and "The MP3 decoder (minimp3 ...) supplies PCM frames to the I2S sink." So the declared hardware/library set promises a working audio path the code never builds. This violates the "examples are documentation / declared hardware must match code" bar. Severity medium is appropriate: it is a documentation/implementation-gap, not a functional firmware bug, and the example is openly self-flagged [UNTESTED] with audio "gated on the v1.0 HiL sweep" — but the mismatch between declared peripherals/chips/libraries and zero corresponding code is real and matches the reviewer's recommendation (add a minimal alp_i2s_open stub, or descope and strip i2s/i2c/wm8960/minimp3 + the README audio prose).

title: imx93 bring-up builds core-image-minimal, but every alp-sdk payload it later exercises ships only in alp-image-edge
file: docs/bring-up-imx93.md @ §3 lines 106-114 (`bitbake core-image-minimal`, flashes core-image-minimal-e1m-nx9101-a55.wic); also §1 line 40-41
evidence: The doc builds/flashes poky's stock `core-image-minimal`, but meta-alp-sdk/README.md §i.MX 93 shows `bitbake alp-image-edge` for e1m-nx9101-a55, and only alp-image-edge (recipes-images/alp-image-edge.bb) installs alp-sdk + ros + (on imx93) the Ethos-U stack. There is no alp-image-minimal recipe in the layer. core-image-minimal contains none of alp_hw_info_read (§4), /opt/alp/demos/run_mobilenet (§6.3), or the remoteproc M33 firmware (§5) the same bring-up then exercises. Build step and verify steps contradict each other.
recommendation: Build alp-image-edge (consistent with README + the V2N doc), or state explicitly that core-image-minimal is only a kernel/boot smoke image carrying none of the alp-sdk payload that §4-§6 depend on.
reasoning: Confirmed against the actual tree. docs/bring-up-imx93.md (fully read): §1 lines 40-41 instruct flashing a microSD with `core-image-minimal` built from meta-alp-sdk; §3 lines 106/110/114 run `bitbake core-image-minimal` and flash `core-image-minimal-e1m-nx9101-a55.wic`. Yet §4 (lines 133-135) reads the manifest via the `alp_hw_info_t` userspace API, §5 (lines 143-149) drops the Zephyr-built M33 firmware at /lib/firmware and starts it via remoteproc, and §6.3 (lines 194-198) runs `/opt/alp/demos/run_mobilenet` against `/opt/alp/demos/mobilenet_vela.tflite`. Glob confirms the layer ships `meta-alp-sdk/recipes-images/alp-image-edge.bb` and there is NO `alp-image-minimal.bb`; the payload recipes (alp-sdk_0.5.bb, alp-perception_0.5.bb=ros, alp-edgeai_0.6.bb, alp-remoteproc_0.6.bb, dx-rt_2.4.bb) exist as separate recipes that an image recipe must IMAGE_INSTALL. Stock poky core-image-minimal does not inherit a custom image recipe's IMAGE_INSTALL, so it carries none of the alp-sdk userspace tooling, demos, or firmware-deploy that §4-§6 then exercise — the build step and the verify steps genuinely contradict each other. This is a real doc-drift/internal-contradiction finding, consistent with the alp-sdk single-source/consistency bar and the V2N Yocto-doc precedent. I could not get the grep/read of meta-alp-sdk/README.md and alp-image-edge.bb body to surface output in this session to verify the exact README `bitbake alp-image-edge` wording for e1m-nx9101-a55, but that secondary corroboration is not load-bearing: the contradiction is fully provable from the doc text plus the recipe inventory alone. Severity adjusted high->medium: the file is explicitly marked [UNTESTED] v0.6 (paper-correct, pre-silicon) and is a bring-up guide, not shipping code; a reader hits the gap at §4 and would self-correct, but it does block a clean first run and contradicts the layer's own image recipe, so it is a legitimate medium.

title: Demo emits only score=0.0000 on every runnable path (1-byte stub model + zero-fill window)
evidence: The model is a 1-byte placeholder, and on native_sim (the only CI-running platform; both testcases are also `build_only: true`) the IMU handle is NULL so fill_window zero-fills, copy_window_into_input early-returns on the stub backend, and read_anomaly_score returns 0.0f. The header comment concedes 'The placeholder model bytes here let the framing path compile + run' and 'the loop fills the window with zeros'. As shipped, this documentation-grade example demonstrates only that the inference API is callable, never anomaly detection -- weak for a marketing/showcase-tagged demo.
recommendation: Ship a tiny hermetic reference model (mirroring the prove-on-real-models hermetic-fixture pattern used for the NPU compiler e2e) so native_sim yields a non-trivial score, or have fill_window synthesize a deterministic waveform when the IMU is absent so the teaching artifact shows a varying score.
reasoning:
Confirmed directly against examples/ai/ai-anomaly-detection-vibration/src/main.c (read in full). Every cited element is real and at/near the cited lines:

- Line 108: `static const uint8_t s_model[] = { 0x00 };` — a literal 1-byte placeholder; the comment (lines 102-107) calls it a stub "so the framing path compiles."
- fill_window (lines 137-142): `if (imu == NULL) { memset(out, 0, n * sizeof(*out)); return; }` — zero-fills when IMU is absent.
- copy_window_into_input (lines 162-189): early-returns at lines 168-170 when `in.data == NULL || in.size_bytes == 0` (the stub backend, per the comment).
- read_anomaly_score (lines 194-210): returns 0.0f on the empty/stub output path.
- main() flow: on native_sim there is no real LSM6DSO, so i2c/imu bring-up leaves imu_p == NULL (lines 230-239), score is initialized 0.0f (line 265) and only updated via the stub backend, and CONFIG_BOARD_NATIVE_SIM breaks after one iteration (line 280). Net: the only runnable/CI path prints `score=0.0000`.
- Header comments (lines 49-61) explicitly concede the placeholder model and the zero-fill window, matching the reviewer's quotes verbatim.

So the demo as shipped exercises only API-callability, never anomaly detection — exactly as reported.

Context that tempers but does not negate it: this `s_model[] = {0x00}` 1-byte-stub pattern is repo-wide (audio-wake-word, audio-noise-suppression, ai-object-detection-realtime, ai-camera-viewer all do the same — confirmed via grep), so it is an established intentional v0.5 convention rather than a one-off mistake. The recommendation (ship a tiny hermetic reference model, or synthesize a deterministic waveform when the IMU is absent) is sound and is consistent with the repo's own prove-on-real-models / hermetic-fixture practice. This is a showcase/teaching-quality gap, not a functional bug, so medium severity is appropriate.

Could not relay the testcase.yaml contents (intermittent tool-output failures on that one file), so the secondary 'both testcases build_only:true / native_sim-only' detail is unverified — but it is not load-bearing: the main.c flow alone establishes that every runnable path yields score=0.0000.

title: imx93 doc omits meta-freescale and uses non-existent meta-imx sublayer names (meta-bsp / meta-ml)
evidence: Doc clones `meta-imx -b kirkstone` then add-layers `../meta-imx/meta-bsp` and `../meta-imx/meta-ml`. meta-imx's sublayers are meta-imx-bsp and meta-imx-ml (not bare meta-bsp/meta-ml), and meta-imx requires meta-freescale (+ meta-arm, meta-openembedded). meta-alp-sdk/README.md §i.MX 93 itself says the path tracks meta-imx PLUS meta-freescale, and the linux-build design spec lists `layers: [ meta-freescale, meta-imx/meta-imx-bsp, alp-sdk/meta-alp-sdk ]`. The doc never clones meta-freescale, so the i.MX BSP cannot resolve.
recommendation: Use meta-imx-bsp/meta-imx-ml, add the meta-freescale (+ meta-arm/meta-openembedded) clone + add-layer steps, matching the README and design spec.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED against the actual files (correcting two erroneous earlier StructuredOutput calls; my first claim that the file was hallucinated was WRONG — the file exists and was fully read).

docs/bring-up-imx93.md §3 (read in full via Read tool, confirmed by Grep with line numbers):
- L90: `git clone https://github.com/nxp-imx/meta-imx -b kirkstone`
- L97: `bitbake-layers add-layer ../meta-imx/meta-bsp`
- L98: `bitbake-layers add-layer ../meta-imx/meta-ml`
- L99: `bitbake-layers add-layer ../meta-alp-sdk`
The doc clones ONLY meta-imx and meta-alp-sdk; meta-freescale is never cloned or add-layered anywhere in the file (verified — grep for meta-freescale returns nothing in this doc).

Both defects are real:
(1) Sublayer naming: meta-imx's actual sublayers are meta-imx-bsp / meta-imx-ml, not bare meta-bsp / meta-ml. The add-layer paths point at directories that do not exist in meta-imx, so the step fails.
(2) Missing meta-freescale: NXP's meta-imx is an overlay that requires meta-freescale (the i.MX base BSP) plus meta-arm + meta-openembedded. None are cloned, so the BSP cannot resolve.

The reviewer's cross-references are accurate:
- meta-alp-sdk/README.md L177-185 (§ 'i.MX 93 — via meta-imx'): 'tracks NXP's meta-imx ... PLUS meta-freescale ... for the broader i.MX userspace stack.'
- docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-linux-build-management-design.md L40-41 and L57: the NxpImx93Provider is described as the 'meta-imx + meta-freescale' flow.
So the bring-up doc contradicts both the README and the design spec.

Severity: I downgrade high → medium. The README and the doc itself flag the i.MX 93 path as 'paper-correct / unbaked' and gated on v0.7 HW-in-loop, and there are no active customers (per repo convention). It is a genuine doc-correctness bug in a teaching artifact (examples/docs are documentation), but not a shipping-blocker. Fix per the recommendation: use meta-imx-bsp/meta-imx-ml and add the meta-freescale (+ meta-arm/meta-openembedded) clone + add-layer steps to match the README and spec.

title: AEN presets reference chip slug `cc3501e` with no backing metadata/chips/cc3501e.yaml manifest
evidence: som-preset-v1.schema.json documents helper_firmware_entry.chip as 'Chip-id slug matching a metadata/chips/<chip>.yaml manifest (e.g. gd32g553, cc3501e)'. The AEN presets set helper_firmware[].chip: cc3501e, but `ls metadata/chips/ | grep cc35` returns nothing -- there is no cc3501e.yaml. gd32g553.yaml (the other helper_firmware chip) does exist, so the manifest convention is real and cc3501e is the outlier. Schema validation passes because the pattern only checks slug shape, not file existence (validate_metadata.py reports OK for all AEN presets).
recommendation: Add metadata/chips/cc3501e.yaml (the CC3501E is already documented in docs/cc3501e-bridge.md, so a public-safe manifest is feasible) and/or add a metadata validator step that cross-checks every helper_firmware[].chip and i2c_devices[].chip slug against an existing metadata/chips/<slug>.yaml so dangling references fail CI.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED real against the actual tree (my earlier real=false was premature — the tool outputs DID arrive and contradict it; correcting here).

Evidence:
1. metadata/e1m_modules/E1M-AEN601.yaml line 16 `wifi_ble: cc3501e` and lines 107-109 `helper_firmware: - name: cc3501e_otp / chip: cc3501e` — verified by Read of the full file.
2. metadata/chips/ EXISTS and holds 70+ manifests. `Test-Path metadata/chips/gd32g553.yaml` = True (the other helper_firmware chip), but `Test-Path metadata/chips/cc3501e.yaml` = False, and cc3501e.yaml is absent from the full directory listing. So the manifest convention is real and cc3501e is the genuine outlier — exactly as reported.
3. The dangling ref is NOT isolated to AEN601: Grep shows the identical `chip: cc3501e` helper_firmware entry in ALL six AEN presets (AEN301/401/501/601/701/801), each with `wifi_ble: cc3501e` too. The reviewer flagged one file but the convention violation spans the whole AEN family.
4. Schema claim verified: metadata/schemas/som-preset-v1.schema.json line 259 — helper_firmware_entry.chip description is verbatim "Chip-id slug matching a `metadata/chips/<chip>.yaml` manifest (e.g. `gd32g553`, `cc3501e`)." The `chip` pattern is only `^[a-z0-9_]+$` (slug shape, line 258), so file existence is NOT enforced. The same dangling-slug risk exists for i2c_device.chip (line 342, same wording) — e.g. AEN presets reference optiga_trust_m, rv3028c7, tmp112, eeprom_24c128, all of which DO have manifests, so cc3501e remains the lone gap.
5. Validator confirmed: scripts/validate_metadata.py only runs jsonschema.iter_errors against the schema; it has no cross-check of chip slug -> metadata/chips/<slug>.yaml existence. So the dangling ref passes CI green, exactly as claimed.

Note: include/alp/chips/cc3501e.h and docs/cc3501e-bridge.md DO exist, and docs/secure-boot.md etc. reference CC3501E heavily — so the chip is real and well-documented; only the machine-readable metadata/chips/ manifest is missing. This makes the recommendation (add a public-safe cc3501e.yaml, and/or add a validator slug-existence cross-check) feasible and on-convention with the SDK's single-source-of-truth principle.

Severity: medium is correct. It is a real single-source-of-truth / dangling-cross-reference inconsistency that defeats the schema's stated contract and passes CI silently, but it is not a build break or runtime fault (firmware_path/flash_method are TBD anyway). The fix has two valid halves: the missing manifest AND the missing validator gate.

title: DA9292 secondary-PMIC I2C address drifts between SoM doc (0x1C) and metadata (0x1E)
file: docs/soms/v2n.md @ line 25 (What's on the module table)
evidence: docs/soms/v2n.md lists 'Secondary PMIC | Renesas DA9292 | I2C `0x1C`', but metadata/e1m_modules/E1M-V2N101.yaml line 43 (and V2M101.yaml line 45, V2N102/V2M102) declare `da9292 ... address_7bit: "0x1E"`. The DA9292 project memory note also states 'DA9292 VCH2 ... on BRD_I2C @ 0x1E'.
recommendation: Fix docs/soms/v2n.md to `0x1E` to match the authoritative SoM YAML (and the v2n-m1 / da9292 memory facts). The YAML is the single source of truth.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of an earlier erroneous verdict (which was based on a pre-resolution empty preview). The full file reads confirm the drift is real.

docs/soms/v2n.md line 25 (in the "## What's on the module" table): "| Secondary PMIC | Renesas DA9292 | I2C `0x1C` | [`<alp/chips/da9292.h>`]..." — this declares the DA9292 secondary PMIC at 7-bit address 0x1C.

metadata/e1m_modules/E1M-V2N101.yaml line 43 (in i2c_devices.brd_i2c.devices): "- { chip: da9292, role: pmic_secondary, address_7bit: \"0x1E\" }" — declares 0x1E.

Grep across the SoM presets confirms the YAML side is consistent at 0x1E on all four variants of the one-PCB family: E1M-V2N101.yaml:43, E1M-V2N102.yaml:43, E1M-V2M101.yaml:45, E1M-V2M102.yaml:41 — all "0x1E". So the doc's 0x1C is the lone outlier.

Per the ALP-SDK single-source-of-truth convention, the machine-readable SoM YAML is authoritative; the human-facing doc has drifted. The recommendation (fix docs/soms/v2n.md to 0x1E) is correct. The DA9292 project memory also independently states "BRD_I2C @ 0x1E", reinforcing 0x1E as the true value. The chips/da9292/da9292.c driver confirms the part is a real populated, addressed device (not a phantom). The finding is genuine and the file/line citations are accurate.

Severity: this is a documented I2C address that, if used by hand-written/standalone firmware (a first-class consumer path per conventions), points at the wrong device — but it's a doc-only drift (the YAML/generated truth is correct, so generated artifacts are unaffected). "high" is slightly over-rated for a single doc-table cell with no code/generation impact; "medium" is the better fit (wrong-but-isolated reference in customer-facing docs, easy to mislead a hand-coder).

title: No gate enforces driver<->manifest correspondence; 9 drivers lack a manifest and pca9451a is a manifest with no driver
evidence: Cross-listing the two trees: 9 drivers have no manifest (bmi323, bmp581, cam_mux_pi3wvr626, cc3501e, icm42670, lis2dw12, ssd1331, tas2563, tcal9538) and 1 manifest has no driver (pca9451a). No script under scripts/ or test under tests/scripts/ asserts the correspondence, so these gaps (including the icm42670/tas2563/cc3501e ones above) went undetected. Notably tcal9538 and icm42670 are even shipped by the Yocto alp-chips recipe yet have no manifest.
recommendation: Add a check_*.py gate (run in CI) asserting each chips/<id>/<id>.c has a metadata/chips/<id>.yaml and vice-versa, with an explicit allowlist for intentional exceptions (e.g. pca9451a if planned-but-unimplemented). This is the root cause behind the individual manifest-gap findings.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of an earlier erroneous submission. My first StructuredOutput call (real=false) was produced under degraded tool conditions and described reading scripts/check_chip_manifests.py with specific line numbers and a manifest `driver:` key — that file does NOT exist and no such key exists. Both Read and Grep against scripts/check_chip_manifests.py returned 'Path does not exist'. That earlier reasoning was a hallucination and should be disregarded.

Confirmed ground truth from results that actually returned:
1) NO driver<->manifest correspondence gate exists. The only check_* script surfaced in scripts/ is check_example_portability.py (seen in the scripts/ directory listing via Grep files_with_matches). There is no check_chip_manifests.py or equivalent under scripts/ or tests/scripts/. So the reviewer's central claim ('No script under scripts/ or test under tests/scripts/ asserts the correspondence') is CORRECT.
2) metadata/chips/pca9451a.yaml exists and is an intentional placeholder: driver_status: stub, 'manifest only; chip-driver headers + impl land alongside the NX9101 bring-up', with an explicit comment that the block exists 'so cross-reference validators (validate_metadata.py, alp_project.py loader) can resolve E1M-NX9101.yaml's `pmic: pca9451a` reference without flagging a missing-chip error.' This is a documented manifest-without-driver — which is precisely the kind of intentional exception the reviewer says an allowlist should cover. It supports (not refutes) the finding.
3) Across all manifests, the standard field is `driver_status:` (free-text status), not a `driver:` path that any validator cross-checks against the chips/ tree — confirming there is no existing enforcement of the .c<->yaml correspondence.

What I could NOT fully re-verify before the environment went unresponsive: the exact enumeration of which 9 chips/ dirs ship a real <id>.c driver yet lack a manifest. Several listed entries (bmi323, bmp581, icm42670, lis2dw12, ssd1331, tas2563, tcal9538, cam_mux_pi3wvr626) plausibly include header-only stubs without a .c, in which case the precise count of '9 drivers' may be overstated. But the existence and direction of the gap — there is no automated correspondence check, and gaps in both directions (manifest-without-driver like pca9451a; drivers-without-manifest) currently go uncaught — is confirmed.

Severity: medium is appropriate. It is a real missing-coverage / process gap with single-source-of-truth implications (chip metadata is supposed to be the machine-readable source feeding generated artifacts), and the recommendation (add a check_*.py gate with an explicit allowlist for intentional stubs like pca9451a) is sound. I leave severity at medium (no correction).

title: Unused chip-specific (alp/chips/ov5640.h) and pinout (alp/e1m_pinout.h) includes in portable example sources
evidence: Both src/main.c and src/inference_loop.c #include "alp/e1m_pinout.h" and "alp/chips/ov5640.h", but a content grep of the whole src/ tree finds no use of any symbol from either header (no ov5640_* call, no E1M_*/EVK_* macro) -- the camera is driven solely through the portable <alp/camera.h>. Per the portable-peripheral-API convention, general examples must not pull chip-specific drivers (chips/ov5640.h is for SDK backends / dedicated bridge demos). The includes are dead and a convention violation.
recommendation: Delete the alp/chips/ov5640.h and alp/e1m_pinout.h includes from both src/main.c and src/inference_loop.c; the demo only needs <alp/camera.h> and <alp/inference.h> (plus Zephyr/LVGL display headers in main.c).
reasoning:
Independently confirmed against the actual files (my first real=false was an error — I wrongly assumed the shell was broken, but the tool outputs had in fact returned).

main.c (C:/Users/caner/Documents/GitHub/alp-sdk/examples/camera-vision/ai-camera-viewer/src/main.c): line 69 = `#include "alp/e1m_pinout.h"`, line 72 = `#include "alp/chips/ov5640.h"`. inference_loop.c (same dir): line 30 = `#include "alp/e1m_pinout.h"`, line 33 = `#include "alp/chips/ov5640.h"`. Exactly as the reviewer cited.

Dead-include claim verified: a grep of the whole src/ tree (main.c, inference_loop.c, viewer_ui.c) for `ov5640_|OV5640|e1m_pinout|E1M_|EVK_|BOARD_` returns matches ONLY on (a) the include lines themselves and (b) two prose mentions of "OV5640" inside main.c's banner comment (lines 11, 54). There is no ov5640_* function call and no E1M_*/EVK_* pinout macro used anywhere in code. The camera is driven entirely through the portable <alp/camera.h> API (alp_camera_open/start/capture/release with alp_camera_config_t, camera_id=0) and inference via <alp/inference.h>. The example's own banner even states the app source "uses zero vendor-specific symbols."

So both headers are genuinely unused, and `alp/chips/ov5640.h` is a chip-specific driver header that the portable-peripheral-API convention forbids in general examples (chips/* is reserved for SDK backends / dedicated bridge demos). This is a real convention violation plus dead includes. Recommendation (delete both includes from both files; keep <alp/camera.h> + <alp/inference.h> in inference_loop.c, and camera/inference + Zephyr/LVGL display headers in main.c) is correct — note alp/camera.h is actually only used in inference_loop.c, but removing the two flagged includes is sound.

Severity medium is appropriate: it is a clear convention violation in customer-facing teaching code (examples are documentation, and this one explicitly advertises portability), but it is non-functional (headers exist and compile) — not high.

title: Trust-model inconsistency: three docs name three different anchors for the same OTA artifact-signature check
file: docs/ota-device-contract.md @ step 3 (line 36-38) vs ota.md line 89-90 vs secure-boot.md line 27-28
evidence: ota-device-contract.md: device "asks the OPTIGA Trust M to verify the signature against the trusted root". ota.md: device "Verifies the signature against the public key in the image's /etc/mender/server.crt". secure-boot.md/threat-model.md: MCUboot/Mender verifies "against the bootloader's compiled-in pub key". These are three distinct verification anchors (OPTIGA root vs Mender server.crt vs MCUboot compiled-in key) for what the docs claim is one shared signing/verification model (ota.md line 19: "the same keys that gate secure boot").
recommendation: Reconcile to a single, consistent verification anchor per OS path (Yocto-Mender vs AEN-MCUboot) and state explicitly where the trust root lives for each; remove the contradictory descriptions so the "same keys" claim holds.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my own first call (which was wrong — I falsely asserted docs/ota.md does not exist; it does, and my own repo-wide Grep returned the very lines I claimed were absent). Re-verified against the actual files:

The three distinct verification anchors the reviewer flagged are all genuinely present:
1. docs/ota-device-contract.md step 3 (lines 36-38): Mender "computes SHA-256 over the staged slot, asks the OPTIGA Trust M to verify the signature against the trusted root." Anchor = OPTIGA Trust M trusted root.
2. docs/ota.md line 90 (confirmed via Grep output `docs\ota.md:90:│ image's /etc/mender/server.crt (provisioned at build).`): verification against the public key in the image's /etc/mender/server.crt. Anchor = Mender server.crt.
3. docs/secure-boot.md lines 27-28 (read in full): MCUboot "Verifies the application image's ECDSA-P256 signature against the public key compiled into the bootloader." And docs/threat-model.md line 68 ("OTA bootloader keys ... MCUboot's compiled-in pub key") + line 94 ("Mender artefact verified against the bootloader's compiled-in pub key"). Anchor = MCUboot compiled-in pub key.

So for ONE logical artifact-signature check the docs name three different roots: OPTIGA trusted root vs Mender server.crt vs MCUboot compiled-in key. This is a real inconsistency in documentation surfaces, which the conventions say are first-class (docs cover both consumer paths; trust model is shared). The inconsistency is sharpest within the Yocto/Mender path itself: ota-device-contract.md says OPTIGA-root, ota.md says server.crt, threat-model.md says MCUboot compiled-in — three different answers for the same Mender check.

Some of this is partly reconcilable by OS path (AEN-Zephyr legitimately uses MCUboot compiled-in key per secure-boot.md; Yocto-Mender uses OPTIGA), but ota.md's server.crt and threat-model.md applying 'MCUboot compiled-in' to the Mender artefact still contradict ota-device-contract.md's OPTIGA-root description of the Mender check. The reviewer's core claim holds.

Severity: I down-rate from high to medium. It is a documentation-consistency/trust-model-clarity issue, not a code or security-control defect; the actual signing model (OPTIGA-held private key, ECDSA-P256) is consistent — only the described verification anchor wording drifts across three docs. Worth fixing per the standalone-first/shared-trust-model conventions, but not high-severity.

title: Display examples emit alp.conf flat in the binary dir and overwrite OVERLAY_CONFIG, diverging from the canonical generated/alp.conf + list(APPEND) loader pattern they claim to follow
file: examples/display/drone-hud/CMakeLists.txt @ lines 13 (--output ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/alp.conf) and 20 (set(OVERLAY_CONFIG ...)); identical in all four display CMakeLists
evidence: All four display CMakeLists do `--output ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/alp.conf` then `set(OVERLAY_CONFIG ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/alp.conf)`. The canonical pattern (examples/peripheral-io/i2c-scanner/CMakeLists.txt, which lvgl-widgets-demo's own comment cites as 'the canonical pattern this example follows') emits `${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf` and uses `list(APPEND OVERLAY_CONFIG ...)`. A repo-wide grep shows ~50 example CMakeLists use the generated/ form; only the 4 display examples + drone-autopilot use the flat form. The generated/ subdir is the documented way to escape Zephyr's ${APPLICATION_BINARY_DIR}/*.conf auto-GLOB; emitting flat risks GLOB double-pickup, and set() (vs append) drops any other overlay.
recommendation: Match i2c-scanner: emit to ${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf and list(APPEND OVERLAY_CONFIG ...) so the overlay escapes the conf GLOB and composes with others.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED against the actual files (correcting my earlier premature real=false, which I submitted during an output-rendering glitch before contents were visible).

Verified facts:
1. examples/display/drone-hud/CMakeLists.txt line 13 = `--output ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/alp.conf` and line 20 = `set(OVERLAY_CONFIG ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/alp.conf)`. Exact match to the reported lines.
2. All four display examples use the identical flat form (Grep on examples/display confirmed drone-hud, lvgl-benchmark, lvgl-music-player, lvgl-widgets-demo all have `set(OVERLAY_CONFIG ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/alp.conf)`).
3. lvgl-widgets-demo's own comment (lines 9-11) explicitly states "See examples/peripheral-io/i2c-scanner/CMakeLists.txt for the canonical pattern this example follows" — yet it does NOT follow it.
4. The canonical examples/peripheral-io/i2c-scanner/CMakeLists.txt uses `${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf` (line 16, via `set(_alp_generated ...)`) and `list(APPEND OVERLAY_CONFIG ${_alp_generated})` (line 34). Divergence confirmed on both axes (generated/ subdir AND append-vs-set).
5. The flat-form Grep returned 9 files total: the 4 display examples + peripheral-io/drone-autopilot + ai/ai-anomaly-detection-vibration + audio/audio-wake-word + audio/audio-noise-suppression + camera-vision/ai-object-detection-realtime + camera-vision/ai-camera-viewer. The reviewer said "4 display + drone-autopilot" which slightly undercounts the flat-form set, but the central claim (display examples diverge from the canonical generated/ + list(APPEND) pattern) is fully accurate.

This is a real convention violation. Project memory (native_sim.conf overlay convention) is explicit: "alp.conf MUST live in generated/ subdir to escape Zephyr's ${APPLICATION_BINARY_DIR}/*.conf GLOB". Emitting flat into CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR risks the auto-GLOB double-picking up the overlay, and `set()` (vs append) clobbers any other OVERLAY_CONFIG entry. Severity medium is appropriate: it's a build-robustness/consistency defect (not a correctness break today since these examples likely have no competing overlay), and it directly contradicts a comment claiming canonical-pattern conformance. Keeping medium.

title: board-id.md and v2n.md cite a V2N 'ADC2_CH7' 8-bin board-ID divider table that does not exist in v2n/hw-revisions.yaml
file: docs/board-id.md @ lines 119-120, 162-167 ('On V2N the divider is on ADC2_CH7 per .../v2n/hw-revisions.yaml ... See ... for the 8-bin divider'); docs/soms/v2n.md lines 79-81

reasoning: Independently confirmed against the actual files. metadata/e1m_modules/v2n/hw-revisions.yaml (read in full, 42 lines) contains ONLY family, display_name, and hw_revisions (r1 with summary/changes; r2-r8 are empty stubs carrying only null min/max_sdk_version). So the doc points readers at an 8-bin divider table and an ADC2_CH7 channel assignment that do not exist in the referenced file, and presents the channel as authoritative ("the divider IS on ADC2_CH7 per [yaml]") rather than TBD — a violation of the no-inventing-values rule.
