title: dx-rt recipe references file://LICENSE with an all-zero checksum but ships no LICENSE file, and its source/sha256 are placeholders — yet it is wired into V2M IMAGE_INSTALL
evidence: LIC_FILES_CHKSUM = "file://LICENSE;md5=00000000000000000000000000000000" but git ls-files recipes-deepx/ shows only dx-rt_2.4.bb (no LICENSE file is shipped, and dx_rt source is private). SRC_URI = "${SP_MIRROR}/dx_rt-2.4.0.tar.gz" (SP_MIRROR undefined in the layer) with SRC_URI[sha256sum] all zeros. do_populate_lic (missing license file), do_fetch (no mirror / bogus hash) all fail. README calls this a 'skeleton', but e1m-v2m101-a55.conf / e1m-v2m102-a55.conf and alp-image-edge.bb IMAGE_INSTALL:append pull 'dx-rt' + 'kernel-module-dx-rt-npu' unconditionally, so a V2M image build references an unbuildable recipe.
recommendation: Mark dx-rt explicitly unbuildable (PNBLACKLIST / EXCLUDE_FROM_WORLD + a do_fetch bb.fatal stub) and gate the V2M IMAGE_INSTALL/dx-rt references behind the same opt-in until the real source, LICENSE file, valid sha256sum, and SP_MIRROR definition land.
reasoning:
CORRECTION OF MY FIRST CALL: my earlier StructuredOutput(real=false) was wrong — it was based on an incomplete/misleading PowerShell `git ls-files` that omitted meta-alp-sdk. Glob and Read confirm the file and the entire Yocto layer genuinely exist.

Verified against the actual file C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\meta-alp-sdk\recipes-deepx\dx-rt\dx-rt_2.4.bb:
- Line 17: LIC_FILES_CHKSUM = "file://LICENSE;md5=00000000000000000000000000000000" — references a LICENSE file with an all-zero md5.
- Lines 23-24: SRC_URI = "${SP_MIRROR}/dx_rt-2.4.0.tar.gz" and SRC_URI[sha256sum] = all zeros (in fact a 65-char string, even malformed). Grep across the whole layer for SP_MIRROR returns only this one use-site (line 23) — it is never defined anywhere in meta-alp-sdk, so do_fetch cannot resolve a URL.
- No LICENSE file is shipped: Glob meta-alp-sdk/recipes-deepx/** shows only dx-rt_2.4.bb; there is no dx-rt/files/LICENSE or recipe-local LICENSE, so do_populate_lic would fail on the file://LICENSE chksum.

Wiring into V2M IMAGE_INSTALL confirmed unconditional and ungated:
- e1m-v2m101-a55.conf lines 21-24 and e1m-v2m102-a55.conf lines 19-22: IMAGE_INSTALL:append includes `dx-rt` + `kernel-module-dx-rt-npu` directly.
- alp-image-edge.bb lines 44-51: IMAGE_INSTALL:append:e1m-v2m101 / :e1m-v2m102 add `dx-rt` + `kernel-module-dx-rt-npu`.
- alp-perception_0.5.bb line 45: RDEPENDS:${PN} = "... dx-rt" — another hard pull.
There is NO PNBLACKLIST / EXCLUDE_FROM_WORLD / BBMASK gating anywhere (grep returned none), so a V2M (e1m-v2m101-a55 / e1m-v2m102-a55) `bitbake alp-image-edge` would reference an unbuildable recipe and fail at do_fetch / do_populate_lic.

The reviewer's description is fully accurate. The README does acknowledge dx-rt is a 'skeleton' (meta-alp-sdk/README.md line 294, "What's deferred"), and the layer is only partially build-validated (core-image-minimal on V2N, not the V2M alp-image-edge path), which mitigates real-world impact somewhat — but the V2M machine .conf + image recipe still hard-wire it with no opt-in guard, exactly as reported. The recommendation (PNBLACKLIST/EXCLUDE_FROM_WORLD + do_fetch bb.fatal stub, gate V2M references behind opt-in) is sound.

Severity: this is dev-state vendor-gated scaffolding that is explicitly documented as deferred, and the V2M alp-image-edge bake is itself an un-reached gate, so it does not break the currently-validated V2N path or shipping artifacts. A V2M consumer build would fail, but the layer README already flags V2M as not-yet-baked. That places it at medium rather than high — a real, correctly-described buildability bug, but in code paths the project has explicitly marked as not-yet-functional.

title: V2N102/V2M102 'larger memory' delta lives only in comments + an unconsumed free-form `memory:` block
file: metadata/e1m_modules/E1M-V2N102.yaml @ memory.dram_mbit/flash_mbit (lines 51-52); cf. E1M-V2N101.yaml lines 51-52 and n44.json variants[3]
evidence: All four V2N SKUs set silicon_variant: R9A09G056N44GBG, resolving to the single matching variant in n44.json (order_code R9A09G056N44GBG, whose alp_module_skus lists all four). The convention says V2N differences are 'only in memory size'. The 32Gbit-vs-64Gbit DRAM/eMMC delta between the 101 and 102 SKUs is expressed only via a free-form `memory: {dram_mbit, flash_mbit}` block (som-preset schema accepts it as additionalProperties:true) plus a trailing comment. A repo-wide grep for `dram_mbit|flash_mbit` finds NO consumer -- no script reads these keys, so the only thing distinguishing the two memory variants is dead, unvalidated, comment-adjacent data (the documented derivation path variants[].sram_banks_kb/mram_mb covers on-die memory, not this external LPDDR4X/eMMC).
recommendation: Give external DRAM/eMMC capacity a typed, validated, actually-consumed home (e.g. a structured external_memory block in the SoM-preset schema that the orchestrator reads) instead of an unconsumed free `memory:` map whose meaning is carried by comments; or, if it is genuinely informational, document it as such so it isn't mistaken for the single source of the 101/102 distinction.
reasoning: Confirmed directly by reading both files. E1M-V2N101.yaml and E1M-V2N102.yaml both set silicon_variant: R9A09G056N44GBG (identical), so they resolve to the same SoC variant. The ONLY substantive structural delta between the two SKUs is the free-form `memory:` block at lines 50-52: V2N101 has dram_mbit/flash_mbit = 32768 with trailing comments "# 32 Gbit LPDDR4X" / "# 32 Gbit eMMC 5.1"; V2N102 has 65536 with "# 64 Gbit LPDDR4X" / "# 64 Gbit eMMC 5.1" (other diffs are just display_name, Yocto machine, Zephyr board, and the header comment "larger memory"). The reviewer's line numbers (51-52) and values are exact. The files' own comment block (lines 75-79) explicitly documents that on-die SRAM/MRAM is derived from the SoC variant JSON via silicon_variant -> variants[].sram_banks_kb/mram_mb, confirming the reviewer's point that the documented single-source-of-truth derivation path covers on-die memory only and does NOT cover this external LPDDR4X/eMMC capacity. Thus the 101<->102 distinction (per the "V2N differences only in memory size" convention) rests entirely on an untyped free-form `memory:` map whose human meaning is carried by trailing comments and which is outside the documented derivation chain -- a genuine SSOT/typed-validation gap. I could NOT independently re-verify the negative half of the claim (that NO script/schema consumes dram_mbit/flash_mbit, schema additionalProperties:true) because tool outputs (Grep/Glob/Bash/PowerShell and later Reads) returned empty for the rest of the session; my partial grep over scripts/ returned no content, consistent with "no consumer," but is not conclusive. The half I verified held exactly as reported. Medium severity is appropriate: this is a metadata-quality/single-source-of-truth concern, not a build break. Files: C:\\Users\\caner\\Documents\\GitHub\\alp-sdk\\metadata\\e1m_modules\\E1M-V2N102.yaml (lines 50-52, 1, 14, 75-79) and ...\\E1M-V2N101.yaml (lines 50-52).

title: linux-renesas bbappend uses the %-wildcard but its DTS patches are pinned to one kernel SHA with no version/SRCREV constraint
evidence: The bbappend is named with the '%' version wildcard, so it attaches to EVERY linux-renesas version, yet its own note says the patches 'were generated at 6717c06c' and asks to 'confirm the patches apply against the linux-renesas SRCREV this layer pins.' Nothing in the layer pins linux-renesas to that SHA. Eight context-based DTS patches with hardcoded base line numbers (e.g. 0008 @@ -617,7, 0006 @@ -112,8) and absolute insertion points (0010 @@ -1559,3 / 0012 @@ -1597,3 / 0013 @@ -1626,3) will fail do_patch the moment the BSP bumps linux-renesas, with no version guard to scope them.
recommendation: Scope the bbappend to the exact kernel PV (e.g. linux-renesas_6.1.%.bbappend) and/or pin/assert the linux-renesas SRCREV the series was generated against.
reasoning: Confirmed against the actual tree (after a flaky-tool retry, the primary Read succeeded). File meta-alp-sdk/recipes-kernel/linux/linux-renesas_%.bbappend exists with the `%` PV wildcard (Glob + Read), so it attaches to EVERY linux-renesas version. Its STATUS note (lines 21-24) reads verbatim: "confirm the patches apply against the linux-renesas SRCREV this layer pins (they were generated at 6717c06c)" — yet the bbappend body contains ONLY FILESEXTRAPATHS:prepend, SRC_URI:append:e1m-v2n101 (8 DTS patches + tas2563-audio.cfg), and a do_configure:append. There is NO SRCREV/LINUX_VERSION/PREFERRED_VERSION_linux-renesas/require anywhere; greps across meta-alp-sdk/ and meta-alp-sdk/conf/ for those returned nothing. So "the SRCREV this layer pins" is aspirational — nothing pins it. The patches are SHA-specific unified diffs applied via do_patch: patch 0008 literally states "CONFIG_REALTEK_PHY=y is already set in the arm64 defconfig at SHA 6717c06c", and the V2N integration doc + superpowers spec/plan repeatedly tie the 0006-0013 series to linux-renesas 6.1.141-cip43 / SHA 6717c06c. Context-based hunks against a moving target will fail do_patch on the next BSP bump with no version guard to scope them. The reviewer's core claim is accurate. I downgrade high->medium for two reasons: (1) the bbappend explicitly self-documents this exact gap in its own STATUS block (a known, called-out risk, not a silent bug), and the whole repo is flagged [UNTESTED]/pre-HIL with this WIRING "not run through bitbake yet"; (2) per the user's auto-memory the patches were just WSL-bake-validated to apply cleanly against the only BSP version that currently exists (v6.30), so today's bake is not broken — the risk is forward-looking (next BSP bump). The recommendation (scope to linux-renesas_6.1.%.bbappend and/or pin/assert the SRCREV) is the correct fix. Note: a couple of Read/grep calls in this session returned empty intermittently (environment flake), but the load-bearing facts were each obtained from at least one successful call against the real files.

title: No native_sim.conf overlays despite examples pulling real-silicon-only Kconfigs; alp.conf not under generated/
evidence: v2n-emmc-block-stat/prj.conf enables CONFIG_DISK_ACCESS=y / CONFIG_DISK_DRIVER_MMC=y / CONFIG_SDMMC_STACK=y, and v2n-xspi-flash-readwrite/prj.conf enables CONFIG_FLASH=y/CONFIG_FLASH_PAGE_LAYOUT=y -- driver-class Kconfigs that typically need a backing device on the target. No example under examples/v2n/ ships a native_sim.conf overlay or a generated/ subdir, and the per-example testcase.yaml files do not wire EXTRA_CONF_FILE=native_sim.conf, contrary to the repo's native_sim.conf-overlay convention for examples that pull real-silicon-only Kconfigs. (alp.conf is emitted to ${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf via CMake, so the GLOB-escape concern is handled, but the missing native_sim overlays remain.)
recommendation: For each v2n example whose prj.conf enables real-silicon-only driver classes, confirm it actually builds on native_sim/native/64; where it does not, add a generated/native_sim.conf overlay wired via testcase.yaml extra_args: EXTRA_CONF_FILE=native_sim.conf (matching the documented convention).
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my earlier premature StructuredOutput (it wrongly said real=false; that was based on Read/Bash returning empty before the slower tools completed — the files DO exist).

Verified facts (from tool results that succeeded earlier in the session):
1. examples/v2n/v2n-emmc-block-stat/prj.conf exists and contains exactly: CONFIG_PRINTK=y, CONFIG_LOG=y, CONFIG_DISK_ACCESS=y, CONFIG_DISK_DRIVER_MMC=y, CONFIG_SDMMC_STACK=y — matching the reviewer's quote verbatim. These are driver-class Kconfigs that need a backing block device.
2. examples/v2n/v2n-xspi-flash-readwrite exists; per examples/README.md it does "Erase + write + read-back one page on the on-module xSPI NOR" (CONFIG_FLASH class).
3. The COMPLETE tracked-file listing (1666 files, fully enumerated via `git ls-files` and a temp-file Read covering all of examples/) shows NO example under examples/v2n/** ships a native_sim.conf, a boards/native_sim_native_64.{conf,overlay}, or a generated/ subdir. Every v2n example contains only CMakeLists.txt / README.md / board.yaml / prj.conf / src/ / testcase.yaml. By contrast, many sibling examples that pull HW-class Kconfigs DO ship these overlays (peripheral-io/i2c-master, spi-*, connectivity/iot-connected-camera, aen/edgeai-vision-aen ship boards/native_sim_native_64.conf; connectivity/iot-dashboard, iot-fleet-ota, production-deployment ship top-level native_sim.conf). So the asymmetry the reviewer describes is real and confirmed.

This matches the documented repo convention (MEMORY: native_sim.conf-overlay convention) that examples pulling real-silicon-only Kconfigs should ship a per-example native_sim overlay wired via testcase.yaml EXTRA_CONF_FILE.

Could NOT verify (search/read tools degraded mid-session and stopped returning content): whether each v2n testcase.yaml restricts builds to real boards via platform_allow — which, if present, would make a native_sim overlay unnecessary and reduce this to a non-issue — and whether the CI native_sim twister scope even includes examples/v2n/. That unknown is load-bearing for severity.

Severity downgraded high->medium: the affected examples are explicitly [UNTESTED] (README/v0.6 status), this is a build-robustness/convention-consistency gap rather than a shipped-functionality defect, and it may be fully masked by platform_allow restricting these to real V2N boards (unconfirmed). The reviewer's recommendation is correctly conditional ("confirm it actually builds on native_sim; where it does not, add an overlay"), which is the right action. The finding's secondary claim about alp.conf is self-conceded as already handled.

title: spi-loopback and spi-master each locally #define ALP_SPI_NO_CS sentinel that the SDK does not export
file: examples/peripheral-io/spi-loopback/src/main.c @ line 25 (#define ALP_SPI_NO_CS 0xFFFFFFFFu); duplicated in spi-master/src/main.c line 59
evidence: Both examples define '#define ALP_SPI_NO_CS 0xFFFFFFFFu' locally, commenting 'Defined locally to keep the example self-contained; future <alp/peripheral.h> revisions may export this as a named constant.' Examples are documentation for hand-written firmware (portable-API convention: app code goes through the <alp/*> surface). A customer copying this gets a magic 0xFFFFFFFF CS sentinel that is NOT part of the public API and could diverge from whatever value the SPI backend actually treats as controller-managed-CS. The value is also duplicated across two examples (single-source-of-truth violation).
recommendation: Export ALP_SPI_NO_CS (or equivalent) from <alp/spi.h>/<alp/peripheral.h> and have both examples include it, instead of each minting its own 0xFFFFFFFF.
reasoning:
I directly read both cited files and confirmed the core facts. C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\examples\peripheral-io\spi-loopback\src\main.c line 25 contains `#define ALP_SPI_NO_CS 0xFFFFFFFFu`, and C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\examples\peripheral-io\spi-master\src\main.c line 59 contains the identical `#define ALP_SPI_NO_CS 0xFFFFFFFFu`. The value is genuinely duplicated across two examples (single-source-of-truth concern) and both are used as `.cs_pin_id = ALP_SPI_NO_CS` in the alp_spi_config_t initializer.

Both comments self-identify the macro as locally defined, and they actually CONTRADICT each other about the SDK surface: spi-loopback says "real apps include this from <alp/peripheral.h>" (implying it exists), while spi-master says "future <alp/peripheral.h> revisions may export this as a named constant" (the author's own admission it is NOT yet exported). That contradiction is itself corroborating evidence that the constant is not reliably part of the public API.

This matches the repo conventions: examples are documentation/teaching artifacts and app code should go through the <alp/*> portable surface; shipping a copy-pasted magic 0xFFFFFFFF CS sentinel that a customer would copy, with no single exported constant, is a real convention/quality finding. Medium severity is right — it is a documentation/convention issue, not a functional bug, and the controller-managed-CS path still works.

Confidence is medium rather than high because my Grep/Glob/Bash/PowerShell tooling returned empty for everything this session (even for strings I had already read in-file), so I could not independently confirm the negative — that <alp/peripheral.h>/<alp/spi.h> does not export ALP_SPI_NO_CS. I rely on the directly-read file contents plus the spi-master comment's explicit "future revisions may export this" admission. The verifiable parts of the reviewer's claim (duplicated local define at the exact cited lines, magic value, convention mismatch) are accurate.

title: mproc-mailbox native_sim testcase omits CONFIG_ALP_SDK_MPROC, unlike its AEN sibling and every RPC example
evidence: The `.aen` test sets `extra_args: CONFIG_ALP_SDK_MPROC=y`; the `.native_sim` test sets no extra_args. prj.conf is 'Empty by design' and there is no per-example native_sim.conf overlay. src/main.c includes <alp/mproc.h> and calls alp_shmem_open / alp_mbox_open / alp_mbox_set_callback, which require the MPROC backend (CONFIG_ALP_SDK_MPROC) compiled in. Every RPC multicore example consistently passes CONFIG_ALP_SDK_RPC=y in its native_sim extra_args (rpmsg-v2n/aen/imx93, heterogeneous-offload); mproc-mailbox is the lone outlier that enables the needed symbol only on the real-silicon variant.
recommendation: Add `extra_args: CONFIG_ALP_SDK_MPROC=y` to the native_sim test (mirroring the .aen variant and the RPC examples), or wire it via a generated alp.conf / native_sim.conf overlay so the symbol is present for the native_sim build.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my premature first submission (my tool results were arriving one batch delayed, not failing). I have now confirmed every claim against the actual files.

CONFIRMED in examples/multicore/mproc-mailbox/testcase.yaml: `.native_sim` test (lines 19-20) carries ONLY `platform_allow: native_sim/native/64` and NO extra_args; `.aen` test (lines 21-25) sets `extra_args: CONFIG_ALP_SDK_MPROC=y` with `build_only: true`. The within-file asymmetry the reviewer flagged is literally present.

CONFIRMED in src/main.c: includes `alp/mproc.h` (line 54) and calls `alp_shmem_open` (101), `alp_mbox_open` (107), `alp_mbox_set_callback` (153), `alp_mbox_send`, `alp_shmem_view` — all of which require the MPROC backend (CONFIG_ALP_SDK_MPROC) compiled in. The native_sim path is NOT fully #ifdef'd out: alp_shmem_open/alp_mbox_open/alp_mbox_send/alp_mbox_set_callback all run unconditionally; only the reply-poll loop is gated behind !CONFIG_BOARD_NATIVE_SIM. So the symbol is genuinely needed for the native_sim build to link.

CONFIRMED prj.conf is "Empty by design" (effective Kconfig = generated build/generated/alp.conf from board.yaml via scripts/alp_project.py over OVERLAY_CONFIG), and the directory listing (Glob) shows NO native_sim.conf overlay — files are CMakeLists.txt, board.yaml, README.md, boards/native_sim_native_64.overlay, peer/, prj.conf, src/, testcase.yaml.

CONFIRMED the RPC siblings are a consistent pattern: rpmsg-v2n (line 36), rpmsg-aen (26), rpmsg-imx93 (25), heterogeneous-offload (26) ALL pass `extra_args: CONFIG_ALP_SDK_RPC=y` on their native_sim test. mproc-mailbox is the lone multicore example whose native_sim variant omits the backend Kconfig that its own real-silicon variant declares.

CONFIRMED board.yaml does NOT explicitly declare an mproc/ipc surface (it has som.sku E1M-AEN701, preset e1m-evk, cores.m55_hp.app ./src, diagnostics only). So whether the generated alp.conf actually enables CONFIG_ALP_SDK_MPROC for the native_sim build depends on orchestrator inference that the four sibling RPC examples deliberately do NOT rely on — they pass the symbol explicitly. This makes the omission a real consistency defect, not merely cosmetic redundancy: either native_sim is genuinely missing the symbol (build/link risk) or the orchestrator supplies it implicitly, in which case the four RPC siblings' explicit extra_args is the documented house convention this file violates.

The finding is real. Severity medium is fair (it is a real inconsistency with a plausible native_sim build-link consequence, in a teaching example), though it leans toward low if the orchestrator already infers the symbol from board.yaml — but since the established sibling pattern is to pass it explicitly, the fix the reviewer recommends (add CONFIG_ALP_SDK_MPROC=y to the native_sim test) is correct and low-risk.

title: V2M machine confs point KERNEL_DEVICETREE at renesas/e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts which no patch produces and the layer doesn't ship; carrier DT patches are V2N101-only
file: meta-alp-sdk/conf/machine/e1m-v2m101-a55.conf @ e1m-v2m101-a55.conf line 45; e1m-v2m102-a55.conf line ~38; bbappend line 36
evidence: Both V2M confs set KERNEL_DEVICETREE:append = " renesas/e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts" but no recipe, patch, or file in the layer creates that dts (the patches only edit r9a09g056n48-rzv2n-evk.dts). Meanwhile the eight carrier DT patches are gated on SRC_URI:append:e1m-v2n101 only; the bbappend concedes 'V2M (DEEPX) SKUs reuse the same deltas ... TODO once those boards are exercised.' V2M builds therefore reference a missing dtb AND get an un-customized kernel — also violating 'V2N family is one PCB; DT customization should be identical across all 4'.
recommendation: Either ship/generate e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts and extend the DT-patch override to the v2m machines (shared family override key), or drop the dangling KERNEL_DEVICETREE line and mark v2m as not-yet-buildable.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual tree via git grep and git ls-files. The cited files and lines are real and accurate:

- meta-alp-sdk/conf/machine/e1m-v2m101-a55.conf:45 = `KERNEL_DEVICETREE:append = " renesas/e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts"` (exact match to the report).
- meta-alp-sdk/conf/machine/e1m-v2m102-a55.conf:36 = the same line (report said line ~38; close enough).
- The string `e1m-x-evk-v2m` occurs ONLY in those two machine confs, the bbappend comment (line 19), and docs/e1m-x-v2n-sdk-integration.md:69. The "any file named that dts" search returned EMPTY — no recipe, patch, or shipped source produces renesas/e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts.
- The eight carrier DT patches (0006-0013) all edit the in-tree r9a09g056n48-rzv2n-evk.dts; none emits a v2m dts.
- meta-alp-sdk/recipes-kernel/linux/linux-renesas_%.bbappend gates everything on the e1m-v2n101 override only: `SRC_URI:append:e1m-v2n101` (line 36) and `do_configure:append:e1m-v2n101` (line 57). Lines 18-19 explicitly concede: "V2M (DEEPX) SKUs reuse the same deltas against a separate e1m-x-evk-v2m.dts target -- TODO once those boards are exercised."

So a V2M (e1m-v2m101/102-a55) build references a dtb that nothing in the layer generates AND, because the patch + config-fragment overrides are e1m-v2n101-only, would receive an un-customized kernel. This also conflicts with the "V2N family is one PCB; DT customization should be identical across all four SKUs" convention.

Severity medium is appropriate: it is a real implementation gap that would break/mis-build the V2M Yocto target, but it is self-documented as a deferred TODO, there are no active customers per repo conventions, and the entire bbappend wiring is already flagged UNVALIDATED-through-bitbake (lines 21-24, 53-56). The fix is exactly the reviewer's recommendation: ship/generate the v2m dts and extend the patch override to the v2m machines via a shared family key, or drop the dangling KERNEL_DEVICETREE line and mark v2m not-yet-buildable.

(Note: my first StructuredOutput call was wrong — I mis-stated the files didn't exist before the tool results came back. The Glob and grep results clearly show they do exist with the cited content.)

title: audio-noise-suppression builds on native_sim but ships no native_sim.conf overlay despite alp.conf pulling real-silicon-only Kconfigs
file: examples/audio/audio-noise-suppression/board.yaml @ cores.m33_sm.libraries (tflite_micro, cmsis_dsp) + peripherals (i2s); testcase platform native_sim/native/64
evidence: board.yaml requests `libraries: [tflite_micro, cmsis_dsp]` and `peripherals: [i2s]`, which alp_project.py turns into CONFIG_I2S=y / CONFIG_CMSIS_DSP=y / TFLM kernels in the generated alp.conf. testcase.yaml builds `alp_sdk.examples.audio_noise_suppression.native_sim` on native_sim/native/64 (build_only). There is no boards/native_sim_native_64.conf and no EXTRA_CONF_FILE/native_sim.conf wiring to neutralise real-silicon-only Kconfigs on native_sim. The documented convention requires examples pulling real-silicon-only Kconfigs via alp.conf to ship a per-example native_sim.conf wired through EXTRA_CONF_FILE. Compare i2s-tone which has boards/native_sim_native_64.overlay.
recommendation: Add a boards/native_sim_native_64.conf (or native_sim.conf + EXTRA_CONF_FILE) that disables/overrides the real-silicon-only symbols so the native_sim build is robust, per the convention used by other examples (e.g. aen/edgeai-vision-aen, i2c-master).
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual files (my first StructuredOutput call was sent before file contents arrived and was wrong; this corrects it).

board.yaml (examples/audio/audio-noise-suppression/board.yaml) declares cores.m33_sm.libraries: [tflite_micro, cmsis_dsp] and peripherals: [i2s] — exactly as the reviewer states (lines 51-59). CMakeLists.txt explicitly documents the generator mapping: peripherals.i2s -> CONFIG_I2S=y, libraries.cmsis_dsp -> CONFIG_CMSIS_DSP=y, libraries.tflite_micro -> CONFIG_ALP_TFLM_REF_KERNELS=y, board.populated.tas2563 -> CONFIG_ALP_SDK_CHIP_TAS2563=y, written to CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR/alp.conf and layered via OVERLAY_CONFIG. So the generated overlay does force on real-silicon-oriented Kconfigs (CONFIG_I2S, CONFIG_CMSIS_DSP).

testcase.yaml defines alp_sdk.examples.audio_noise_suppression.native_sim on platform_allow native_sim/native/64, build_only: true — confirmed.

Glob confirms the example ships NO boards/ directory at all: no native_sim_native_64.conf and no .overlay, and no EXTRA_CONF_FILE / native_sim.conf wiring. By contrast its sibling audio example i2s-tone ships boards/native_sim_native_64.overlay, and edgeai-vision-aen / i2c-master / spi-* / timer-periodic-interrupt ship boards/native_sim_native_64.conf — corroborating that examples pulling I2S/real-silicon Kconfigs neutralise them for native_sim. This matches the documented convention (examples pulling real-silicon-only Kconfigs via alp.conf must ship a per-example native_sim overlay).

So the gap the reviewer flags is real: CONFIG_I2S=y (no native_sim I2S device) and CONFIG_CMSIS_DSP=y are emitted into the native_sim build with nothing to override them, unlike every comparable example. This is a build-robustness/coverage gap, not a runtime-correctness bug, so medium severity stands.

Caveat lowering confidence to medium: I did not execute alp_project.py to dump the exact generated alp.conf, nor run twister, so I'm relying on the CMakeLists-documented mapping rather than observing CONFIG_I2S=y in the produced file or a confirmed native_sim build failure. The convention also prescribes generated/ + EXTRA_CONF_FILE, which differs slightly from the reviewer's boards/*.conf RECOMMENDATION, but that is a fix-shape detail; the underlying missing-coverage finding is valid.

title: Silicon part number drift: integration doc + errata title say RZ/V2N 'r9a09g056n48', metadata says 'N44'
file: docs/e1m-x-v2n-sdk-integration.md @ line 8 ('Renesas RZ/V2N (r9a09g056n48; ...)'); docs/errata-e1m-x-v2n.md line 1 title ('r9a09g056n48')
evidence: docs/e1m-x-v2n-sdk-integration.md line 8 and docs/errata-e1m-x-v2n.md line 1 both say `r9a09g056n48`. But metadata/socs/renesas/rzv2n/n44.json declares `"ref": "renesas:rzv2n:n44"`, `"part": "R9A09G056N44"`, every SoM YAML uses `silicon: renesas:rzv2n:n44` + `silicon_variant: R9A09G056N44GBG`, and docs/soms/v2n.md / docs/boards/e1m-x-evk.md / docs/bring-up-v2n.md all say `R9A09G056N44`. The '48' suffix appears only in these two docs.
recommendation: Reconcile to one canonical part number. If the bench silicon is genuinely the N48 variant, the SoC JSON + every SoM YAML are wrong (single-source-of-truth violation); if N44 is correct, fix the integration doc and errata title. Either way the docs and metadata must agree.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual files. docs/e1m-x-v2n-sdk-integration.md line 3 reads "RZ/V2N (r9a09g056n48; AI SDK platform 7.1...)" and docs/errata-e1m-x-v2n.md line 1 title reads "Errata — E1M-X-EVK carrier + V2N-M1 SoM (RZ/V2N r9a09g056n48)". Both use the n48 suffix as the silicon part number. Meanwhile the canonical metadata says N44: metadata/socs/renesas/rzv2n/n44.json line 6 "part": "R9A09G056N44GBG"; all four SoM YAMLs (E1M-V2N101/102, E1M-V2M101/102) declare silicon_variant: R9A09G056N44GBG; and docs/soms/v2n.md, docs/boards/e1m-x-evk.md, docs/portability-matrix.md, zephyr/Kconfig, vendors/renesas-rzv2n/README.md all say R9A09G056N44(GBG#AC0). So the two flagged docs genuinely contradict the single-source-of-truth part number — the finding is real.

Two corrections to the reviewer: (1) the integration-doc occurrence is at line 3, not line 8. (2) The reviewer's claim that "the 48 suffix appears only in these two docs" is wrong — r9a09g056n48 appears extensively across the Yocto/BSP layer (docs/build-yocto-v2n.md, meta-alp-sdk machine confs, kernel patches 0006-0013, the .bbappend, audio-noise-suppression/testcase.yaml). However, those are NOT drift: there r9a09g056n48-rzv2n-evk.dts/.dtb is the literal upstream Renesas BSP filename / DT compatible string for the RZ/V2N EVK that the production bootloader loads by name; reconciling those to n44 would break the build. Also note N48 (R9A09G056N48GBG) is a real sibling order_code listed in n44.json variants[] line 166, just not the ALP default (#AC0 = N44, per the JSON note line 204).

Net: real part-number drift in exactly the two flagged docs, but it is a doc-only wording inconsistency on an [UNTESTED] v0.6 branch, not a code/metadata/build defect, and the fix is a 2-line s/n48/n44 in prose (the BSP-filename n48 usages must be left alone). I down-rate from high to medium: it's a credible single-source-of-truth consistency violation worth fixing, but it does not affect generated artifacts, builds, or runtime, and the surrounding BSP filenames legitimately retain n48.

title: Alif E8 flags pending_alif_datasheet:true while its datasheet block + notes assert a released primary v1.0 — contradictory provenance
file: metadata/socs/alif/ensemble/e8.json @ pending_alif_datasheet: true (line 8) vs datasheet v1.0 / doc_id ADTS0013 (lines 9-14) and notes[0] 'Datasheet v1.0 released ... supersedes the prior v0.51' (line 240)
evidence: The schema defines pending_alif_datasheet:true as 'populated from secondary sources ... Replace with primary datasheet data before locking for production.' Yet e8.json simultaneously carries a full primary datasheet block (version 1.0, doc_id ADTS0013, 2026-05-11) and notes[0] stating the v1.0 datasheet is released and supersedes v0.51, plus 'Peripheral counts above were ingested against v0.51 and remain provisional'. So the boolean says 'no primary datasheet yet' while the content says 'primary v1.0 ingested (peripheral counts still provisional)'. E6/E7 do not set this flag despite the same posture, so a consumer gating on it cannot tell whether E8 is primary or secondary.
recommendation: Make the flag truthful: clear pending_alif_datasheet now that v1.0 exists and instead use a narrower marker for the still-provisional peripheral counts (the v0.51->v1.0 reconcile), so the boolean isn't both 'released' and 'pending'.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual file metadata/socs/alif/ensemble/e8.json. Lines 7-14 show: "status":"released", "pending_alif_datasheet": true (line 8), immediately followed by a populated PRIMARY datasheet block: "version":"1.0", "extracted_at":"2026-05-11", "source":"Alif_E8_Datasheet_v1.0.pdf", "doc_id":"ADTS0013" (lines 9-14). This is the exact contradiction the reviewer flagged: the boolean meaning "no primary datasheet / preliminary, replace with primary-source values" coexists with a fully populated primary v1.0 datasheet block.

The documented semantics of the flag corroborate the contradiction: docs/porting-new-som.md says to "Replace pending_alif_datasheet ... with primary-source values once the silicon ships" (line 194) and that it "Surfaces a non-fatal WARN ... so reviewers know the values are preliminary" (line 211); the schema (soc-spec-v1.schema.json line 37+) defines the flag with a sibling rationale field for what is pending. CHANGELOG.md:5051 also ties the flag to the v0.51 supersession narrative. So a primary v1.0 datasheet is asserted as ingested while the flag still claims preliminary/pending — genuinely contradictory provenance.

I could not re-open the notes[0] line text in this session (Read calls in later turns returned empty due to a tooling hiccup), so I did not byte-verify the exact "supersedes the prior v0.51 ... peripheral counts remain provisional" wording at line 240; but that wording is corroborated by CHANGELOG.md:5051 ("E8 supersedes the v0.51") and is not load-bearing for the core finding, which is fully proven by lines 7-14 alone. Re e6/e7: grep for pending_alif_datasheet returned only e8.json and e5.json, so e6/e7 indeed omit the flag (consistent with the reviewer's point that a consumer gating on the flag can't distinguish primary vs secondary across the family) — note e5 also sets it, so e8 is not the sole case.

Severity medium is correct: it is a non-fatal, WARN-driving metadata provenance inconsistency that can mislead a consumer gating on the boolean, not a build break. The recommendation (clear the flag now that v1.0 is ingested, and use a narrower marker for the still-provisional v0.51-era peripheral counts) is sound and aligns with the single-source-of-truth convention.

title: V2M101 capability key 'deepx_dx: true' uses the de-canonicalised DEEPX_DX name
evidence: The V2M101 preset declares the DEEPX accelerator capability as 'deepx_dx: true' while the same file's on_module.npu and inference.preferred_backend both use the canonical 'deepx_dxm1' (lines 31, 60). The capability flag thus carries the old DEEPX_DX spelling the rename was meant to retire (canonical is DEEPX_DXM1, not DEEPX_DX). A capabilities consumer keying on deepx_dx vs deepx_dxm1 will see two names for one feature within a single file.
recommendation: Rename the capability key to deepx_dxm1 (matching npu/preferred_backend) across V2M101/V2M102, or confirm the capabilities namespace deliberately differs and document why.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual file C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\metadata\e1m_modules\E1M-V2M101.yaml. (My earlier real=false call was made before the tool results returned and is superseded by this one.)

Direct evidence from the file:
- Line 31: `npu: deepx_dxm1` (canonical spelling)
- Line 60 (under `inference:`): `preferred_backend: deepx_dxm1` (canonical spelling)
- Line 63 (under `capabilities:`): `deepx_dx: true   # DEEPX DX-M1 NPU on-module via PCIe.` (de-canonicalised DEEPX_DX spelling)

So within a single file the same DEEPX DX-M1 feature is named two ways: `deepx_dxm1` in npu/preferred_backend and `deepx_dx` in the capabilities map. The repo convention (per CLAUDE.md memory and the supplied review bar) is that DEEPX_DXM1 is canonical and the DEEPX_DX→DEEPX_DXM1 rename was specifically done to retire the old `deepx_dx` spelling. The capabilities key carries the retired name.

The issue is NOT isolated: grep shows the identical drift in the sibling SKU E1M-V2M102.yaml (line 27 `npu: deepx_dxm1`, line 56 `preferred_backend: deepx_dxm1`, line 59 `deepx_dx: true`). Per the V2N-family "one PCB, variant-populated" rule the two are expected to match — and they do match each other, but both carry the stale key. The other 9 module YAMLs (AENxxx, NX9101, V2N101/102) do not declare a DEEPX capability, so this is confined to the two DEEPX-populated SKUs.

The reviewer's line-number citation (63), file, and the npu/preferred_backend cross-reference are all accurate. This is a genuine naming-drift finding. Severity medium is appropriate: it is real intra-file inconsistency with the established canonical-name rule and would split any capabilities consumer keying on `deepx_dx` vs `deepx_dxm1`, but it is metadata-only (no build break observed) and the comment makes the intent unambiguous, so not high. The reviewer's recommendation to rename the key to `deepx_dxm1` across V2M101/V2M102 (or document a deliberate capabilities namespace) is correct.
