title: CC3501E brownout trip voltage contradicts between the two CC3501E docs (~1.3 V vs 1.6 V vs 1.71 V)
file: docs/cc3501e-bridge.md @ line 66 (reset-cause table) vs docs/cc3501e-integration-plan.md §3.9.1 lines 680, 685-687
evidence: cc3501e-bridge.md line 66: 'Brown-out (VDDMAIN ~1.3 V trip) | Cold-boot'. cc3501e-integration-plan.md §3.9.1 line 680 table: 'Brownout detection (VDDMAIN ~1.6 V)'; its prose lines 685-687: 'The brownout threshold is VDDMAIN < 1.71 V (SWRU626 §7.1.4) -- the 1.3 V number cited in docs/cc3501e-bridge.md Table is from the RVM comparators, which trip first.' Three numbers (1.3, 1.6, 1.71 V) describe the same brownout event; the integration plan explicitly flags the bridge doc as wrong yet the bridge doc was never corrected.
recommendation: Correct docs/cc3501e-bridge.md brownout row to VDDMAIN < 1.71 V (SWRU626 §7.1.4) and move the ~1.3 V figure to the RVML/RVMH row. Align cc3501e-integration-plan.md §3.9.1's table (1.6 V) with its own prose (1.71 V).
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my prior call: the tool outputs were delayed, not empty — they all returned and I have now read the actual files. The finding is fully confirmed.

docs/cc3501e-bridge.md line 66 (reset-cause table, "How the chip decides what to do at reset"): "| Brown-out (VDDMAIN ~1.3 V trip)   | Cold-boot |".

docs/cc3501e-integration-plan.md §3.9.1 ("Reset sources", header at line 674):
- line 680 table row: "| Brownout detection (VDDMAIN ~1.6 V)| Device AON Reset | Yes — uncatchable, but firmware can report on next PING |"
- lines 685-687 prose: "The brownout threshold is **VDDMAIN < 1.71 V** (SWRU626 §7.1.4) — the 1.3 V number cited in `docs/cc3501e-bridge.md` Table is from the RVM comparators, which trip first. Both reset the device cold."
- line 992 checklist further reinforces 1.71 V: "[ ] Brownout (§7.1.4) — confirm 1.71 V trip; report on next PING."

So there are genuinely three different figures (1.3 V in the bridge doc, 1.6 V in the plan's own table, 1.71 V in the plan's own prose) for the same brownout event. The integration plan explicitly calls the bridge doc's 1.3 V wrong (attributing it to the RVM comparators), yet:
1. The bridge doc was never corrected — it still says ~1.3 V for the Brown-out row (line 66), even though it has a separate RVML/RVMH row (line 65) where the 1.3 V comparator figure belongs.
2. The integration plan is internally inconsistent — its table (1.6 V, line 680) contradicts its own prose (1.71 V, lines 685-686) and its own checklist (1.71 V, line 992).

The reviewer's evidence, line numbers, quoted strings, and recommendation all match the real tree exactly. This is a genuine documentation inconsistency across two docs plus an internal one within the integration plan.

Severity: medium is appropriate. It's customer-facing reference documentation (CC3501E bring-up is a real consumer path) and an electrical spec value, so it is more than cosmetic, but it's doc-only with no code/build impact and the brownout-detection behavior itself is unaffected by the cited number. Not high (no functional/safety regression in shipped code), not low (it's a contradicted hardware electrical fact that the docs themselves flag as wrong and a customer could mis-set a brownout assumption). Keeping medium.

title: architecture.md 'how a block resolves to a backend' describes an obsolete src/<os>/i2c.c → vendors/<som>/i2c.c dispatch flow that no longer exists
file: docs/architecture.md @ lines 327-356 ('Peripherals: how a block resolves to a backend')
evidence: The doc shows `alp_i2c_open(cfg) → src/<os>/i2c.c → vendors/<som>/i2c.c → vendor HAL`. On disk the actual architecture is a linker-section backend registry: dispatch lives in `src/i2c_dispatch.c` and per-vendor backends live under `src/backends/i2c/<vendor>.c` (confirmed: `src/` contains `i2c_dispatch.c`+`backend.c`+30 `*_dispatch.c`; `src/backends/` has per-class subdirs). `src/zephyr/` contains only `inference_tflm.cpp`+README and `vendors/alif/` contains only notes/README — there is no `src/zephyr/i2c.c` nor `vendors/<som>/i2c.c`. The registry architecture is correctly documented in `docs/architecture/backend-registry.md`, so architecture.md contradicts its own sibling doc.
recommendation: Rewrite the 'how a block resolves to a backend' section to describe the `src/<class>_dispatch.c` + `src/backends/<class>/<vendor>.c` registry (`ALP_BACKEND_REGISTER`, `alp_backend_select()` tiebreaker), or replace it with a pointer to docs/architecture/backend-registry.md.
reasoning:
I directly read C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\docs\architecture.md lines 310-379 and independently confirmed the load-bearing half of the claim: the "Peripherals: how a block resolves to a backend" section (lines 327-356) contains the exact obsolete flow quoted by the reviewer — a code block showing `alp_i2c_open(cfg) → src/<os>/i2c.c  # picks the OS path → vendors/<som>/i2c.c  # picks the vendor HAL call → vendor HAL → CMSIS → silicon`, with `src/common/` described as OS-agnostic glue. This is verbatim what the reviewer reported.

Corroborating evidence within the SAME file: line 323 (Inference row) describes a "Registry-backed dispatcher + the backend-registration seam," i.e. a registry model, while the peripherals section describes a per-directory src/<os>/ + vendors/<som>/ dispatch model. That is an internal contradiction in architecture.md itself, which independently supports a drift finding regardless of on-disk layout. The MEMORY/conventions context also establishes the linker-section backend registry (ALP_BACKEND_REGISTER, alp_backend_select) as the real architecture.

LIMITATION: My follow-up tool calls to verify the on-disk side (src/i2c_dispatch.c, src/backends/i2c/<vendor>.c, absence of src/zephyr/i2c.c and vendors/<som>/i2c.c, and the sibling docs/architecture/backend-registry.md) all returned empty output due to a tool-output rendering failure this turn, so I could not file-confirm the "no longer exists on disk" half. The quoted obsolete doc text, the in-file self-contradiction with the registry-based Inference row, and the established registry architecture make the finding genuine.

Severity: this is pure documentation drift in an architecture overview — misleading but not build/API breaking. The reviewer's "high" is slightly over-rated; medium is more appropriate, kept meaningful by the self-contradiction with the sibling registry doc. Recommendation (rewrite to the src/<class>_dispatch.c + src/backends/<class>/<vendor>.c registry, or point to backend-registry.md) is sound.

title: DEEPX example docs call the host compiler 'dx-compiler' / 'dx-com' instead of the canonical 'dxcom'
file: examples/v2n/v2n-m1-deepx-inference/README.md @ line 71-72 ('use DEEPX's `dx-compiler` toolchain to produce a `.dxnn`'); echoed in src/main.c header comment ('a DXNN-compiled model (from DEEPX's compiler toolchain)')
evidence: README line 71: 'Replace `k_placeholder_model` in `src/main.c` with your own DXNN-compiled model (use DEEPX's `dx-compiler` toolchain to produce a `.dxnn` file from a TFLite source) before flashing.' The canonical host compiler is `dxcom` (per scripts/alp_model/adapters/deepx.py: console script `dxcom`, `dxcom -m model.onnx -c config.json -o out/`). Also note 'from a TFLite source' is wrong: deepx.py accepts only ONNX (`accepts()` returns src_format == 'onnx'); dxcom is an ONNX frontend, not TFLite.
recommendation: Replace 'dx-compiler' with 'dxcom' in the README and the main.c comment, and correct the input format from TFLite to ONNX (dxcom ingests ONNX, not TFLite).
reasoning:
CORRECTION: My first StructuredOutput call fired on a momentarily empty tool buffer; the parallel Read calls actually succeeded and I have now independently confirmed the finding against all three files.

VERIFIED in examples/v2n/v2n-m1-deepx-inference/README.md (read directly):
- Lines 70-72: "Replace `k_placeholder_model` in `src/main.c` with your own DXNN-compiled model (use DEEPX's `dx-compiler` toolchain to produce a `.dxnn` file from a TFLite source) before flashing." Both defects present verbatim: (1) name is `dx-compiler`, not the canonical `dxcom`; (2) input is described as "a TFLite source".

VERIFIED canonical truth in scripts/alp_model/adapters/deepx.py (read directly):
- Module docstring line 5-8: console script is `dxcom`; invocation `dxcom -m <model.onnx> -c <config.json> -o <output_dir>`; "ONNX frontend".
- accepts() line 58-59: `return src_format == "onnx"  # dxcom is an ONNX frontend` — confirms ONNX-only, so "TFLite source" is wrong.
So both halves of the reviewer's claim (wrong name + wrong input format) are real and the canonical references cited are accurate.

PARTIAL CORRECTION to the reviewer on the second locus: the src/main.c header comment does NOT contain the wrong name. main.c lines 31-34 read "on real hardware you'd swap in a DXNN-compiled model (from DEEPX's compiler toolchain) and your domain payload." That phrasing ("DEEPX's compiler toolchain") is generic and correct-ish; it does NOT say "dx-com" or "dx-compiler" and does NOT mention TFLite. The reviewer quoted it accurately ("a DXNN-compiled model (from DEEPX's compiler toolchain)") but characterized it as an "echo" of the error — it is not an error and needs no change. The only file requiring a fix is the README (lines 71-72). The reviewer's RECOMMENDATION to also edit the main.c comment is therefore unnecessary/incorrect for that line.

The README error is a genuine canonical-name drift (dx-compiler vs dxcom) plus a factual input-format error (TFLite vs ONNX), in a customer-facing teaching artifact (examples are documentation). Per the conventions, canonical-name drift (DEEPX_DXM1 / dxcom / dxnn) is a real finding. Severity medium is appropriate-to-slightly-generous: it is doc-only, in a build_only HiL example that returns NOSUPPORT under native_sim, so no build/runtime breakage — but it would mislead a customer into using the wrong tool and wrong source format. I'd rate it low-to-medium; keeping medium is defensible given customer-facing + factual-wrongness, so I leave it at medium.

Note: other examples already use the canonical surface correctly (e.g. v2n-m1-ros-perception, ai-object-detection-realtime use `.dxnn` / ALP_INFERENCE_MODEL_DXNN), and notably examples/camera-vision/ai-object-detection-realtime/README.md line 78 uses `dx_compiler --target dx-m1 ...` which is ALSO non-canonical (separate finding, out of scope here).

title: local-ci.md hardcodes the maintainer's Documents/GitHub path layout and leads with the slow /mnt/c anti-pattern
evidence: Repeatedly prescribes `/mnt/c/Users/<you>/Documents/GitHub/alp-sdk` and `C:\Users\<you>\Documents\GitHub\zephyrproject` — the exact ...\Documents\GitHub\... layout that is the maintainer's real cwd (C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk). Step 4 (L63-74) makes running twister against /mnt/c/... the primary documented path, while cross-platform-setup.md §5.3 (L404) explicitly says **Avoid** keeping the workspace under /mnt/c/Users/... (~10x slower DrvFs I/O) and local-ci.md's own 'What's slow on WSL' (L95-102) concedes the /mnt/c path is slow and recommends cloning into ~/. The recommended path and the anti-pattern are inverted.
recommendation: Use a neutral placeholder (e.g. ~/dev/alp-sdk or /path/to/alp-sdk) and lead with the native-ext4 (~/) workspace as primary, matching cross-platform-setup.md §5.3. Per the 'no local paths' convention, doc-prescribed paths should not mirror the maintainer's machine layout.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual file C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\docs\local-ci.md. The cited path strings exist exactly as reported: L65 and L73 and L84 all use `/mnt/c/Users/<you>/Documents/GitHub/alp-sdk` (the maintainer's real ...\Documents\GitHub\... layout; real cwd is C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk), L133 uses `C:\Users\<you>\Documents\GitHub\zephyrproject`, and L143-156 use `C:\Users\<you>\...` paths. Grep independently confirmed the three /mnt/c hits at L65/73/84.

The inversion claim is also accurate. Path A Step 4 (L63-74) and Step 5 (L81-90) make running twister against `/mnt/c/Users/<you>/Documents/GitHub/alp-sdk` the PRIMARY documented flow (EXTRA_ZEPHYR_MODULES and the `cd /mnt/c/...` in the twister invocation). Yet the same file's 'What's slow on WSL' section (L95-102) concedes /mnt/c reads over the 9P bridge add ~5-10s/build and recommends `git clone` into WSL's `~/` for the native-side clone. And cross-platform-setup.md §5.3 L404 explicitly states 'Avoid: keeping the workspace under /mnt/c/Users/... ~10x slower DrvFs I/O'. So the doc leads with the very anti-pattern its sibling doc says to avoid.

Two nuances that don't negate the finding but temper it: (1) paths use the `<you>` placeholder rather than a hardcoded literal `caner`, so it is not a raw leak of the maintainer's username — but it still mirrors the maintainer's exact directory layout (Documents/GitHub/<repo>), which the 'no local paths / don't mirror maintainer machine layout' convention targets. (2) Step 3 (L53-61) does correctly clone Zephyr into `~/zephyrproject` with a note about native ext4 speed, so the doc is not uniformly wrong — but the alp-sdk checkout itself is still documented primarily on /mnt/c.

Severity 'medium' is appropriate: it's a documentation convention/consistency issue (slow anti-pattern promoted as primary + maintainer layout mirrored), not a code or security defect. Not low because it actively misdirects contributors toward the ~10x-slower path that two other doc locations warn against.

title: All four display examples route the ST7789 panel over EVK_SPI_BUS_ARDUINO, which EVK metadata documents as terminating on the on-module CC3501E Wi-Fi chip, not the SoC
file: examples/display/lvgl-widgets-demo/board.yaml @ pins[] E1M_SPI1 (line 17); identical in drone-hud/board.yaml:25, lvgl-benchmark/board.yaml:15, lvgl-music-player/board.yaml:14
evidence: Every display board.yaml declares the display bus as `{ e1m: E1M_SPI1, macro: EVK_SPI_BUS_ARDUINO, doc: "Arduino UNO header SPI; terminates on the on-module CC3501E, not the main SoC" }`. metadata/boards/e1m-evk.yaml:145-146 confirms EVK_SPI_BUS_ARDUINO == E1M_SPI1 with that exact doc. READMEs claim the ST7789 is wired here (lvgl-widgets-demo/README.md:18-19 'ST7789 TFT panel wired to: SPI1 -> display SCLK + MOSI + CS'). A SPI bus that terminates on the on-module CC3501E cannot reach an external display panel. This is copy-paste of the Arduino-header SPI route into display examples where it is physically wrong.
recommendation: Point the display examples at the EVK board macro actually routed to the display/TFT header and fix the doc/README wiring. If no SoC-direct display SPI exists on the EVK, say so rather than claim the Arduino/CC3501E bus drives the panel.
reasoning: CORRECTION of my earlier premature call (it fired before tool output rendered; the reads/greps did return). Verified against the real tree. (1) examples/display/lvgl-widgets-demo/board.yaml:17 literally contains `{ e1m: E1M_SPI1, macro: EVK_SPI_BUS_ARDUINO, doc: "Arduino UNO header SPI; terminates on the on-module CC3501E, not the main SoC" }`, and the identical line appears in drone-hud/board.yaml:25, lvgl-benchmark/board.yaml:15, lvgl-music-player/board.yaml:14 (all 4 display examples) — citations exact. (2) metadata/boards/e1m-evk.yaml:145-146 confirms EVK_SPI_BUS_ARDUINO == E1M_SPI1 with the exact CC3501E-termination doc; the generated header include/alp/boards/alp_e1m_evk_routes.h:66 and CHANGELOG:6438 ("CC3501E-mediated") corroborate. (3) lvgl-widgets-demo/README.md:18-19 claims the 240x320 ST7789 TFT is wired to "SPI1 -> display SCLK + MOSI + CS". A SPI bus the EVK's own single-source-of-truth metadata documents as terminating on the on-module CC3501E Wi-Fi chip cannot reach an external display panel, so the four display examples assert a physically impossible wiring. The EVK declares no SoC-direct external-SPI display route (only E1M_SPI1=Arduino/CC3501E; defaults.display is the ssd1306 I2C OLED), so the recommendation to repoint at a display-routed macro may need the "no SoC-direct display SPI exists, say so" branch. The finding is genuine and accurately described. I lowered severity high->medium: examples are teaching artifacts marked [UNTESTED] with no active customers, no build break, and the underlying macro is correctly defined and legitimately reused by the spi-* peripheral examples; the defect is a doc/example wiring contradiction that misleads hardware bring-up rather than a runtime/security bug.

title: SWD clock signal name drifts: docs + most metadata say GD32_SWCLK, but renesas-peripheral-map says GD32_SWDCLK
file: metadata/e1m_modules/v2n/renesas-peripheral-map.tsv @ line 60 (GD32_SWDCLK P71) vs gd32-io-mcu-map.tsv line 3, gd32g553.yaml lines 31/63, docs/gd32-bridge-protocol.md §10, docs/gd32-bridge.md Flashing table
evidence: renesas-peripheral-map.tsv line 60: 'GD32_SWDCLK\tP71' (and renesas-peripheral-map.csv line 60 'GD32_SWDCLK,P71'). Every other source uses GD32_SWCLK: gd32-io-mcu-map.tsv 'GD32_SWCLK\tPA14', gd32g553.yaml 'GD32_SWCLK (P71; ...)', the V2N CHANGELOG, docs/gd32-bridge-protocol.md §10 ('GD32_SWDIO + GD32_SWCLK + GD32_NRST'), and docs/gd32-bridge.md ('Renesas-side software SWD controller drives GD32_SWDIO + GD32_SWCLK'). Single-source-of-truth violation: the same net has two spellings across machine-readable metadata.
recommendation: Rename GD32_SWDCLK to GD32_SWCLK in renesas-peripheral-map.tsv + .csv so all metadata and docs agree on one canonical signal name.
reasoning: Confirmed against the actual files. metadata/e1m_modules/v2n/renesas-peripheral-map.tsv line 60 reads exactly "GD32_SWDCLK\tP71" (verified by Read of the full file: line 59 GD32_SWDIO P70, line 60 GD32_SWDCLK P71, line 61 GD32_NRST P74). The .csv sibling matches: renesas-peripheral-map.csv line 60 = "59,GD32_SWDCLK,P71". Every other V2N source for the same net uses the spelling GD32_SWCLK: gd32-io-mcu-map.tsv line 3 "GD32_SWCLK\tPA14", metadata/chips/gd32g553.yaml lines 31 and 63 (GD32_SWCLK, P71), metadata/chips/gd32_swd.yaml, metadata/e1m_modules/v2n/CHANGELOG.md, include/alp/chips/gd32_swd.h (table maps GD32_SWCLK P71<->PA14), docs/gd32-bridge.md, and docs/gd32-bridge-protocol.md §10 (719/744). A repo-wide grep shows the ONLY other "SWDCLK" hit is metadata/e1m_modules/aen/from-alif.tsv "JTAG_TCK/SWDCLK" — an unrelated Alif AEN module net, not the V2N GD32 clock — so it does not dilute the finding. This is a genuine single-source-of-truth violation: the same physical net (Renesas P71, GD32 PA14, host-driven SWD clock) carries two spellings across machine-readable metadata, and the .tsv/.csv pair is the outlier vs all docs and other metadata. Severity medium is appropriate: it is a naming-drift/consistency bug in canonical metadata (could mislead a generator or a reader cross-referencing nets) but does not change pin assignment (P71 is consistent everywhere) and is not a runtime/safety defect. Recommendation to rename GD32_SWDCLK -> GD32_SWCLK in both renesas-peripheral-map.tsv and .csv (line 60) is correct and low-risk. Note: the env returned grep/read output normally; an earlier StructuredOutput call I made claiming I could not read files was premature and is superseded by this confirmed verdict.

title: a55_cluster slices declare app-level Linux peripheral lists inconsistently across the multicore examples
evidence: rpmsg-v2n/board.yaml gives the a55_cluster `peripherals: [ethernet, usb, emmc]` plus an `iot:` block, while rpmsg-aen/board.yaml and rpmsg-imx93 omit any a55/a32 peripheral list. Linux-side peripherals are DT/BSP-determined, not app-declared (the carrier/BSP owns ethernet/usb/emmc), so declaring them on one Linux slice but not the others is drift and blurs whether these are app-meaningful.
recommendation: Standardize whether the Linux (A-cluster) slice should declare peripherals at all and apply the same convention across rpmsg-v2n, rpmsg-imx93, rpmsg-aen, and heterogeneous-offload.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual files (full reads returned). examples/multicore/rpmsg-v2n/board.yaml lines 26-34: the a55_cluster (Linux/Yocto) slice declares `peripherals: [ethernet, usb, emmc]` plus an `iot:` block (wifi/mqtt/tls). examples/multicore/rpmsg-aen/board.yaml lines 40-48: the a32_cluster (Linux/Yocto) slice declares NO `peripherals:` and no `iot:` block — only app, image, libraries. So the central claim is real: the Linux A-cluster slice carries an app-level peripheral list in rpmsg-v2n but not in the sibling rpmsg-aen example. This is genuine cross-example drift.

The reviewer's premise is also consistent with alp-sdk conventions: Linux-side ethernet/usb/emmc are carrier/BSP/DT-determined, not app-meaningful declarations, so listing them on one Linux slice and not the other blurs intent. Note ethernet/usb/emmc are real V2N carrier facts (cf. examples/v2n/v2n-ethernet-dual, v2n-emmc-block-stat), but whether they belong as a per-core app `peripherals:` list on the Linux slice is the inconsistency in question, not their existence.

I could not get fresh tool output for rpmsg-imx93/board.yaml and heterogeneous-offload/board.yaml this turn (those reads returned no content), so I cannot independently re-confirm the reviewer's specific characterization of those two files. However, the v2n-vs-aen divergence alone substantiates the finding's core. Severity correctly rated low: this is a cosmetic/consistency/altitude issue (app-declaring BSP-owned Linux peripherals) with no functional impact. Confidence medium because two of the four cited files were re-confirmed directly and the iot: block on the V2N Linux slice is an additional, related app-vs-BSP smell the recommendation should also cover.

title: Mender artifact private signing key referenced as an in-repo keys/ path, contradicting the "private keys never live in git" trust model
evidence: ota.md board.yaml example: "signing_key: keys/mender_artifact.pem". keys/README.md line 5-6 states "Production keys never live in git" and keys/.gitignore blocks all `*.pem`. ota.md presents a private artifact-signing key as a tracked-looking repo path with no note that it is dev-only/gitignored, unlike the MCUboot dev key which is clearly documented as gitignored. ota.md line 19 also claims OTA uses "the same keys that gate secure boot" (which never leave OPTIGA), yet here a separate file-based Mender signing key is introduced.
recommendation: Either reconcile the Mender artifact signing with the OPTIGA-anchored ECDSA-P256 key (matching the "same keys" claim) or explicitly document `keys/mender_artifact.pem` as dev-only and gitignored, mirroring the mcuboot_dev key treatment; do not present a private signing key as a committable repo path.
reasoning:
CORRECTION: My first StructuredOutput call was wrong — I fired it on incomplete Glob results before the parallel Grep/PowerShell results returned. The files DO exist. Confirmed by direct reads:

1. docs/ota.md exists; line 45 literally contains `signing_key: keys/mender_artifact.pem` inside the board.yaml `ota:` example (read the full file). Line 19 claims OTA uses "the same keys that gate secure boot." The same `signing_key: keys/mender_artifact.pem` string also appears in docs/board-config.md:1010 and docs/tutorials/12-mender-ota.md:74; board.schema.json:290 defines the `signing_key` field. So the cited path is real and repeated across docs.

2. The keys/ scaffold exists as tracked files: keys/README.md, keys/.gitignore, keys/generate_dev_key.sh (confirmed via git ls-files and Glob).

3. docs/secure-boot.md (read in full) corroborates the trust model the reviewer cites: the MCUboot dev key keys/mcuboot_dev_ecdsa_p256.pem is explicitly annotated "(gitignored)" (line 79); the production private key "never exists outside the OPTIGA Trust M's secure NVM" (line 127). It links to keys/README.md as the key-files reference.

The reviewer's substance holds: ota.md presents a PRIVATE Mender artifact-signing key (keys/mender_artifact.pem) as a plain repo-looking path with no "(gitignored)"/dev-only annotation, whereas the analogous MCUboot dev key IS clearly marked gitignored in secure-boot.md — a real asymmetry. And line 19's "same keys that gate secure boot" (OPTIGA-anchored, never-leave-the-chip) sits awkwardly with introducing a separate file-based Mender key. This is a genuine doc-consistency inconsistency.

CAVEAT: A harness fault prevented me from re-confirming the exact wording of keys/README.md ("Production keys never live in git") and keys/.gitignore (`*.pem` block) on this pass (many Read/Bash/PowerShell/Grep calls returned no result this turn). I confirmed those files exist and that secure-boot.md independently documents the gitignored-dev-key / OPTIGA-prod-key model, which is why I rate confidence medium not high.

SEVERITY DOWNGRADE to low: this is a documentation-annotation gap, not a security defect — no private key is actually committed (the keys/ scaffold gitignores key material per the secure-boot.md model). The fix is a one-line "(dev-only, gitignored)" note mirroring the MCUboot dev-key treatment, or reconciling the "same keys" wording. Medium overstates it.

title: dac-waveform expresses DAC enablement via the `adc` peripheral token because board.yaml schema has no `dac` token
evidence: board.yaml comment: 'The DAC half of <alp/adc.h> is enabled by the `adc` peripheral token (no separate `dac` token in the board.yaml schema today).' A DAC-only example must declare `adc` to obtain DAC support. The declarative single-source-of-truth board.yaml therefore does not faithfully express what the app uses (DAC, not ADC); scripts/alp_project.py PERIPHERAL map confirms an 'adc' key but no 'dac' key.
recommendation: Add a `dac` peripheral token to the board.yaml schema + alp_project.py mapping so DAC-only projects declare what they actually use, rather than overloading `adc`.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual tree. examples\peripheral-io\dac-waveform\board.yaml lines 33-38 contain exactly the quoted comment ("The DAC half of <alp/adc.h> is enabled by the `adc` peripheral token (no separate `dac` token in the board.yaml schema today).") and declares peripherals: [adc] under cores.m33_sm. scripts/alp_project.py line 687-688 _PERIPHERAL_KCONFIG maps "adc": "ADC" and has no "dac" key. So the reviewer's factual claims are accurate: a DAC-only example declares `adc` to obtain DAC support, and there is no dac token.

However the severity rating is appropriate-to-generous. This is an intentional, in-file-documented design decision, not drift: DAC and ADC are both surfaced through a single header <alp/adc.h> ("both are analog converters from the SDK's perspective"), so the `adc` peripheral token enabling the shared analog-converter Kconfig is by design. The single-source-of-truth concern is real but minor — the board.yaml still declares the analog-converter subsystem the app uses; it just doesn't sub-distinguish DAC vs ADC because the SDK API doesn't either. The recommendation (add a dac token) is a reasonable nicety but optional. Real but low severity, bordering on intentional/won't-fix.

Note: my first StructuredOutput call (real=false) was made under the mistaken belief that all tools had returned empty output; the tool results actually arrived and confirm the finding. This corrected submission reflects the verified facts.

title: E1M-X-EVK audio mclk.rate_hz emitted as a fact while commented 'confirm against the ADG / clock-gen setup'
evidence: Line 209: `rate_hz: 12288000  # 256 x 48 kHz (default -- confirm against the ADG / clock-gen setup)`. Per line 193 the audio block feeds the generated Linux DT (rcar_sound + audio-graph-card), so an unconfirmed MCLK rate becomes a hard DT value. The 'confirm' hedge indicates the single source of truth is not yet authoritative.
recommendation: Confirm the MCLK rate against the SoM ADG/clock-gen and drop the hedge, or gate the audio DT emission behind partial_hw_config until confirmed.
reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual file C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\metadata\boards\e1m-x-evk.yaml (my first StructuredOutput was made before the Read returned; the file exists and I have now read it in full).

Line 209 is verbatim: `rate_hz: 12288000             # 256 x 48 kHz (default -- confirm against the ADG / clock-gen setup)`. The "confirm against the ADG / clock-gen setup" hedge is real and lives directly on an emitted value.

The downstream-DT claim is also accurate: the audio-block header comment (lines 192-196) states "Scripts that emit the Linux DT (rcar_sound + audio-graph-card) read this block", so `mclk.rate_hz` does flow into a generated DT as a hard value. Per the single-source-of-truth convention, an unconfirmed/hedged value in the authoritative YAML that becomes a generated DT fact is a legitimate inconsistency finding.

Severity remains low (correct): (1) the value is a standard, sane default (256 x 48 kHz = 12.288 MHz, the canonical I2S MCLK), so the emitted DT is very likely correct; (2) the surrounding block is already explicitly marked preliminary -- `status.preliminary: true` and `status.partial_hw_config: true` (lines 254-255), plus board-wide "pending HW-writeup ratification" notes. The hedge is consistent with the board's declared preliminary status, so this is doc/precision drift, not a functional bug. The reviewer's own recommendation (gate behind partial_hw_config) is partially already satisfied since partial_hw_config is true; the actionable residue is just dropping the inline "confirm" hedge once verified. No over/under-rating; low is appropriate.

title: Self-contradictory "Truncated SHA-256" crypto description in GD32 OTA contract
evidence: "* Truncated SHA-256 (full hash, not truncated; the "truncated" refers to the build-id macro which is 20 ASCII hex chars = 10 bytes of hash)." The bullet label contradicts its own parenthetical.
recommendation: Rename the bullet to "Full SHA-256 image hash" and describe the 10-byte build-id separately, so the heading no longer says "Truncated" when the hash is full.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED against the actual file. docs/ota-device-contract.md lines 120-122 (under "### Crypto", line 116) read verbatim:

"* Truncated SHA-256 (full hash, not truncated; the "truncated"\n  refers to the build-id macro which is 20 ASCII hex chars =\n  10 bytes of hash)."

The bullet label "Truncated SHA-256" is immediately contradicted by its own parenthetical "(full hash, not truncated...)". This is a genuine, self-contradictory, confusing doc wording bug exactly as the reviewer described. The reviewer's quote, file path, and line numbers all match. (My initial tool calls did in fact return output; I confirmed the text via Read of lines 105-144 and two Greps that pinpointed lines 120 and 121.)

ADDITIONAL finding the reviewer MISSED that makes the recommendation slightly wrong: the rest of the codebase consistently describes the build-id as a truncated SHA-1, NOT a hash of the image at all:
- firmware/gd32-bridge/src/protocol.c:49 "Build identifier. Linker-emitted, ASCII hex truncated SHA-1 of"
- docs/gd32-bridge-protocol.md:51 GET_BUILD_ID returns "build_id:char[20] (truncated SHA-1, ASCII hex)"
- include/alp/chips/gd32g553.h:153 & :343 "truncated SHA-1 build-id (ASCII hex, NUL-terminated)"
- examples/v2n/v2n-gd32-bridge-ping (main.c:85, README.md:14) "truncated SHA-1 of the bridge"

So the build-id is a SHA-1-based build identifier (a VCS/build fingerprint), conceptually distinct from the image integrity hash. The OTA Crypto bullet conflates two different things: the image-integrity hash (which the doc itself and line 123's "ECDSA-P256 signature over the SHA-256" imply is a full SHA-256) and the 20-char build-id (which is SHA-1 per the rest of the tree, not "10 bytes of hash" of the SHA-256). The reviewer's recommendation to "rename to Full SHA-256 image hash and describe the 10-byte build-id separately" is directionally correct but should also fix the SHA-1-vs-SHA-256 drift in the parenthetical.

Severity: agree with low. This is doc-stale/cosmetic wording in a forward-looking, [UNTESTED] v0.6 contract doc (README line 5 marks the whole SDK paper-correct, no real-silicon verification); the OTA path itself is explicitly future/TBD (e.g., line 113 "N TBD pending bootloader implementation", line 133 "that's a v0.5 problem"). No code or build is affected. Low is correct.

title: alp.conf overlay emit path differs: three examples use CMAKE_BINARY_DIR/generated, iot-dashboard uses CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR/generated
file: examples/connectivity/iot-connected-camera/CMakeLists.txt @ line 16 set(_alp_generated ${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf); cf iot-fleet-ota line 17, production-deployment line 13, iot-dashboard line 18
evidence: iot-connected-camera (line 16), iot-fleet-ota (line 17) and production-deployment (line 13) emit to ${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf; iot-dashboard/CMakeLists.txt line 18 uses ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf and is the only one carrying the long rationale comment (lines 9-17) about burying alp.conf in generated/ to escape Zephyr's ${APPLICATION_BINARY_DIR}/*.conf GLOB. Under sysbuild/twister CMAKE_BINARY_DIR and CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR can differ, and the overlay-ordering protection rationale exists in only one of the four files.
recommendation: Use one consistent variable (CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR/generated/alp.conf) across all four CMakeLists.txt and carry the GLOB-trap rationale comment in each so the protection is uniform.
reasoning:
Verified by directly reading all four CMakeLists.txt files. Every factual claim holds:
- examples/connectivity/iot-connected-camera/CMakeLists.txt line 16: set(_alp_generated ${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf)
- examples/connectivity/iot-fleet-ota/CMakeLists.txt line 17: set(_alp_generated ${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf)
- examples/connectivity/production-deployment/CMakeLists.txt line 13: set(_alp_generated ${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf)
- examples/connectivity/iot-dashboard/CMakeLists.txt line 18: set(_alp_generated ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/generated/alp.conf), and it alone carries the long GLOB-trap rationale comment (lines 9-17 explaining the ${APPLICATION_BINARY_DIR}/*.conf merge-config trap that the native_sim.conf MBEDTLS override depends on).

So the inconsistency is real: three siblings use CMAKE_BINARY_DIR, one uses CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR, and the protective rationale is documented in only one of four. A secondary inconsistency the reviewer didn't mention also exists: the three append (list(APPEND OVERLAY_CONFIG ...)) while iot-dashboard overwrites (set(OVERLAY_CONFIG ...)).

Impact assessment confirms low, not higher: all four place alp.conf in the generated/ subdir, so the load-bearing protection (per MEMORY: alp.conf must live in generated/ to escape the GLOB) is intact in every file. The variable choice is secondary. These are standalone single-app examples (no sysbuild present), so in the application's CMake scope CMAKE_BINARY_DIR == CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR — there is no functional difference as built. The reviewer's "can differ under sysbuild/twister" caveat is technically true for general CMake but does not bite these non-sysbuild examples, so it slightly overstates risk. Net: a genuine, minor consistency/maintainability finding (single-source-of-truth spirit — same job done four different ways, protective rationale in only one). Low severity is correct.

Note: Grep and Bash tool calls returned empty output for unrelated reasons in this session, but the Read tool gave full authoritative file content, which is the primary evidence.

title: heterogeneous-offload README claims a shared imu_fft.h-style header but the wire contract is duplicated inline in each main.c
evidence: README says method names + payload shapes are agreed 'via the shared `imu_fft.h`-style declaration inline in each main.c'. There is no shared header; both linux/src/main.c and m33_sm/src/main.c independently redefine FFT_LEN/MAGS_LEN (linux lines 32-33, m33_sm lines 40-41). The phrase 'shared ... declaration inline in each main.c' is self-contradictory and there is no imu_fft.h in the tree, so the contract can silently drift between the two slices.
recommendation: Either factor the FFT_LEN/MAGS_LEN/method-name contract into one shared header included by both slices (true single source of truth) or correct the README to state the contract is duplicated by hand in each main.c and must be kept in lockstep.
reasoning: Confirmed against the actual file. I fully read examples/multicore/heterogeneous-offload/README.md; lines 35-36 contain the exact flagged, self-contradictory phrasing: "Method names + payload shapes (both sides agree on these via the shared `imu_fft.h`-style declaration inline in each main.c)". A thing cannot be both a "shared declaration" and "inline in each main.c". I independently confirmed there is NO imu_fft.h (nor any other shared header) anywhere in the repo: git ls-files (1665 tracked files) lists the heterogeneous-offload example as containing only board.yaml, README.md, CMakeLists.txt, linux/CMakeLists.txt, linux/src/main.c, m33_sm/CMakeLists.txt, m33_sm/prj.conf, m33_sm/src/main.c, m33_sm/testcase.yaml — no header file at all — and a Select-String across all tracked files for "imu_fft" returned no matches other than the README prose itself. So the example genuinely has two independent slices (linux/src/main.c and m33_sm/src/main.c) with the wire contract necessarily duplicated by hand in each, and the README mislabels it as a single "shared" source of truth. This is a real inconsistency and a legitimate drift risk that conflicts with the alp-sdk single-source-of-truth + examples-are-documentation conventions. NOTE: a persistent harness issue returned empty output for many content-bearing Read/Grep/Bash/PowerShell calls this turn, so I could NOT re-confirm the precise line numbers of the FFT_LEN/MAGS_LEN #defines the reviewer cited (linux 32-33, m33_sm 40-41); however, the existence/absence facts that make the finding true do not depend on those exact line numbers. Severity low is appropriate: it is a documentation-wording nit in an example explicitly marked [UNTESTED] / v0.6 paper-correct, not a build or runtime defect.

title: drone-hud advertises a lock-free 'volatile snapshot' concurrency model, but g_telem is non-volatile and read via a non-atomic struct copy written by two threads
file: examples/display/drone-hud/src/main.c @ g_telem (line 62), snapshot copy (line 130); main.c header lines 32-34; sensors.h lines 7-8
evidence: Header + sensors.h assert 'single-writer + single-reader per field, so a plain volatile snapshot read from main is correct without locks.' But `static drone_telemetry_t g_telem;` has no volatile qualifier and main does `drone_telemetry_t snapshot = g_telem;` -- a non-atomic aggregate copy of multi-word float fields concurrently written by the imu_loop and slow_telem threads. The described 'volatile snapshot' is neither volatile nor atomic; per-field tearing is possible. The comment over-promises correctness the code does not provide and teaches an unsafe pattern in a documentation-grade example.
recommendation: Drop the 'volatile'/'correct without locks' wording, or back it with the real primitive (per-field atomics, seqlock, or double-buffer) and describe the actual concurrency contract.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my earlier premature real=false (that verdict was issued during a burst of timed-out/empty tool results; the Read of the actual file in fact succeeded with full content). Verified directly against examples/display/drone-hud/src/main.c and sensors.h.

Confirmed facts:
- main.c lines 32-34 header comment: "Shared state is the `drone_telemetry_t` struct in sensors.h -- single-writer + single-reader per field, so a plain volatile snapshot read from main is correct without locks." Exactly as the reviewer quoted.
- Line 62: `static drone_telemetry_t g_telem;` — NO volatile qualifier, as claimed.
- Line 130 (inside the while(1) render loop): `drone_telemetry_t snapshot = g_telem;` — a non-atomic aggregate struct copy, as claimed.
- Two threads write g_telem concurrently: imu_entry (line 73, drone_sensors_run_imu_loop @ ~100Hz, K_PRIO_PREEMPT(2)) and telem_entry (line 85, drone_sensors_run_slow_loop @ 5Hz, K_PRIO_PREEMPT(6)); main reads it. So it is multi-writer + single-reader of the aggregate.
- Lines 125-128 comment further claims the once-per-frame snapshot means "a partial sensor update mid-render doesn't show a torn value" — which is false for a plain non-atomic multi-word struct copy that can itself be preempted by the higher-priority IMU thread mid-copy.

Technical assessment: the reviewer is correct. The comment promises a concurrency guarantee the code does not provide. (a) "volatile" is asserted but g_telem is not volatile. (b) Even if it were volatile, volatile does NOT provide atomicity in C — a struct copy of multi-word float fields (attitude quaternion/floats) is decomposed into multiple loads/stores and can be preempted by the higher-priority imu_loop, yielding per-field tearing (mixing old/new floats). (c) The "snapshot ... doesn't show a torn value" comment compounds the over-promise. This is a documentation-grade teaching example (examples are documentation per repo convention), so an incorrect concurrency pattern being taught is a legitimate finding.

One minor refinement to the report: the reviewer once says "single-reader" but the writers are TWO threads (imu + slow telem); the header's own "single-writer per field" claim is about disjoint fields, which is plausible per-field but irrelevant to the aggregate non-atomic copy issue. This does not weaken the finding.

Severity: low is appropriate. It is a teaching/documentation correctness issue in a paper-correct/UNTESTED v0.6 example (README line 5 marks the whole SDK [UNTESTED]); the over-promise teaches an unsafe pattern but causes at most occasional cosmetic UI tearing of a HUD value, no memory unsafety, and the example never runs on real silicon yet. Keeping low.

title: iot-connected-camera EVK overlay ships invented raw SoC gpio bank/pin numbers as 'placeholders' instead of generated board routes
evidence: The overlay hardcodes <&gpio0 0..28>/<&gpio1 0..1> bank/pin numbers and the NOTE (lines 72-78) admits they are PLACEHOLDERS pending the alp_e1m_evk_v2n board file from alplabai/alp-zephyr-modules. The convention is that a carrier wires only to E1M connector IOs and the authoritative bank/index mapping is generated, not hand-placed; shipping invented <&gpioX Y> columns risks a customer copying non-authoritative routing even though the HW-in-loop test is parked (testcase.yaml lines 28-32).
recommendation: Make the placeholder gpio columns obviously fake (e.g. a single sentinel bank) or move them behind the parked test, and remove once the generated board file lands; keep the positional index as the stable ABI.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my premature first call (the tools did return content; I wrongly assumed they were broken). I have now read the actual files.

CONFIRMED FACTS in C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\examples\connectivity\iot-connected-camera\boards\alp_e1m_evk_v2n.overlay:
- Lines 38-67 hardcode raw bank/pin columns: 28 entries on <&gpio0 0..28> plus two on <&gpio1 0> (line 42) and <&gpio1 1> (line 67).
- The header comment (lines 32-35) AND the trailing NOTE (lines 71-79) BOTH explicitly state the <&gpioX Y> bank/pin numbers are PLACEHOLDERS pending the authoritative alp_e1m_evk_v2n board file in alplabai/alp-zephyr-modules.
- testcase.yaml lines 28-32 confirm the HW-in-loop evk_v2n variant is parked (commented out), only native_sim builds.

So the reviewer's factual description is accurate, not hallucinated.

HOWEVER, severity is correctly LOW and arguably should stay low / borderline non-issue, because the file already implements much of the reviewer's own RECOMMENDATION: (1) the overlay is only consumed by the PARKED test (it targets the alp_e1m_evk_v2n board which has no board file, so it cannot build today — testcase.yaml does not enable it), (2) the comments are unambiguous that the columns are TBD placeholders and that only the POSITIONAL INDEX is the stable ABI, (3) the file documents the exact upstream source that will supersede it. The residual risk (a customer copying non-authoritative <&gpioX Y> routing) is real but heavily mitigated by the loud in-file warnings and the fact the overlay is dead until the board file lands. This is a documentation/hygiene nit, not a correctness or single-source-of-truth violation in the generated-metadata sense (this is a hand-written EVK overlay re-aliasing a board file, which is the sanctioned pattern; the bank/pin numbers are the only invented part and are flagged as such). Low is the right rating.

title: Captured camera frame is copied into s_frame but never fed to inference; decode emits a hardcoded box
file: examples/camera-vision/ai-camera-viewer/src/inference_loop.c @ s_frame decl line 52; memcpy line 106; invoke line 118; synthetic box lines 126-130
evidence: The loop memcpy's the captured frame into s_frame (line 106) but s_frame is never read again. alp_inference_invoke(inf) (line 118) runs with no input copy (TODO line 115), and state->boxes[0] is unconditionally set to a fixed rectangle (x=60,y=60,w=120,h=120,score=0.92f, lines 126-130) regardless of any model output. So the entire capture->infer->decode path is non-functional even on real silicon -- it always shows one fixed box. This is an acknowledged v0.5 stub, but the README/main.c headline ('run real inference on AEN', 'bounding boxes on the on-die NPU') overstates what executes.
recommendation: Either wire alp_inference_get_input()/get_output() so the frame is actually fed and real output decoded, or soften the README/comment so it is explicit that no real detection occurs yet and the box is a fixed placeholder.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my earlier (premature) StructuredOutput call: that call wrongly claimed the file did not exist, based on Glob/find timeouts — but my Read of examples/camera-vision/ai-camera-viewer/src/inference_loop.c actually SUCCEEDED and returned the full 148-line file. The file is real and every cited line matches exactly:
- Line 52: `static uint8_t s_frame[240 * 240 * 2] __aligned(16);` (captured-frame buffer).
- Line 106: `memcpy(s_frame, frame.data, n);` inside the camera-capture branch. s_frame is a file-scope static that is written here but never read anywhere afterward — confirmed by reading the whole function.
- Lines 113-118: a TODO comment states verbatim "copy s_frame into the model's input tensor via alp_inference_get_input(), then invoke. v0.5 just times the empty invoke so the UI shows the latency floor," followed by `(void)alp_inference_invoke(inf);` with no input copy.
- Lines 123-130: `state->n_boxes = 1; state->boxes[0] = (viewer_box_t){.x=60,.y=60,.w=120,.h=120,.class_id=0,.score=0.92f};` set unconditionally regardless of model output, with comment "v0.5 emits a synthetic box ... v0.6 wires the real post-process."
So the capture->infer->decode path is genuinely non-functional: the frame is copied but never fed to the model, the invoke runs on an empty/placeholder model (s_model[] = {0x00}), and a single fixed box is always emitted. The defect is exactly as described. It IS an explicitly acknowledged v0.5 stub (multiple inline TODO(v0.6) comments), and examples are documentation/teaching artifacts in this repo, so 'low' severity is appropriate — it is not a shipping-product correctness bug. I could not re-confirm this turn whether the README/main.c 'headline' overstates the behavior (Read/Grep on README.md and main.c returned no output in the follow-up calls), but that is the soft 'overstatement' half of the finding; the core technical defect in inference_loop.c is fully verified against the actual file. corrected_severity stays low.

title: getting-started.md hardcodes '11 minimal apps' which disagrees with the actual tree and tutorials/README
evidence: Line 414: 'examples/ -- 11 minimal apps, one per <alp/*.h> class'. examples/peripheral-io alone holds 18 directories (adc-voltmeter, can-loopback, dac-waveform, drone-autopilot, gpio-button-led, hello-world, i2c-master/scanner/slave, pwm-led-fade, qenc-readout, spi-loopback/master/slave, uart-echo/hello-world/rx-ringbuf, vendor-ext-composability), and the tree has many more categories. tutorials/README.md L12-13 separately says '27 examples'. The hardcoded counts disagree with each other and the tree.
recommendation: Drop the hardcoded count and point to examples/README.md (the live catalogue), or recompute. Hardcoded inventory counts are recurring drift after the reorg.
reasoning: Confirmed against the actual file. docs/getting-started.md line 413-415 reads: "**Per-peripheral examples**: [`examples/`](../examples/README.md) -- 11 minimal apps, one per `<alp/*.h>` class, each driven by a matching `board.yaml`." (verified via Read and Grep, line 414 contains "11 minimal apps, one per `<alp/*.h>` class"). This hardcoded count is stale: examples/peripheral-io alone contains 18+ example directories (adc-voltmeter, can-loopback, dac-waveform, drone-autopilot, gpio-button-led, hello-world, i2c-master/scanner/slave, pwm-led-fade, qenc-readout, spi-loopback/master/slave, uart-echo/hello-world/rx-ringbuf, vendor-ext-composability), and the doc itself references additional category subdirs (examples/aen/, examples/connectivity/, examples/v2n/) — far more than 11. The reorg into category subdirs (per repo memory, 43 flat examples → 8 category subdirs in PR #82) makes "11 minimal apps, one per <alp/*.h> class" factually wrong. This is a genuine doc-stale finding. Severity low is appropriate: it is a cosmetic/inventory-count drift in a 'where to go next' pointer section, not a correctness or build issue, and the link to examples/README.md (the live catalogue) is already present, so the recommendation (drop the count, point to the catalogue) is sound. The cross-doc disagreement with tutorials/README's '27 examples' is secondary but reinforces that hardcoded counts drift. I confirmed the load-bearing claim (the literal stale string in getting-started.md) directly; that alone establishes the finding as real.

title: E1M-X-EVK audio block lists concrete shutdown/fault E1M pads but comments them as still-pending, leaving an ambiguous source of truth
evidence: Lines 216-217 comment "Shared amp control lines ... exact E1M pads pending (maintainer assigning)" yet lines 220-221 assign concrete pads `e1m: E1M_X_I2S1_SCLK` and `e1m: E1M_X_I2S1_SDI`, with line 221 further hedging "confirm SDI is I2S1 (NOT the live I2S0 audio data line)". A scripts-consumed metadata block (line 193 says scripts emit the Linux DT from it) should not carry values flagged as unconfirmed/pending, since the DT generator will emit them as fact.
recommendation: Mark these pads explicitly unresolved (e.g. omit them or set a TBD sentinel the generator skips) until ratified, or remove the 'pending/confirm' hedges once verified. Don't ship generator-consumed pads that the comments say are unverified.
reasoning:
Verified directly against C:\Users\caner\Documents\GitHub\alp-sdk\metadata\boards\e1m-x-evk.yaml (read lines 185-234). Every quoted detail checks out: line 193 states scripts emit the Linux DT (rcar_sound + audio-graph-card) from this block; lines 216-217 comment "Shared amp control lines ... exact E1M pads pending (maintainer assigning)."; lines 220-221 nonetheless assign concrete pads `e1m: E1M_X_I2S1_SCLK` (shutdown) and `e1m: E1M_X_I2S1_SDI` (fault_irq); line 221 further hedges "confirm SDI is I2S1 (NOT the live I2S0 audio data line)". The contradiction is in fact sharper than reported: lines 218-219 also say "Amp control on the otherwise-unused E1M_X_I2S1 pads ... Wired on current PCB rev." which directly contradicts the line 216-217 "pending (maintainer assigning)" hedge. So a generator-consumed metadata block carries concrete values that its own adjacent comments flag as unverified/pending — a genuine ambiguous-source-of-truth inconsistency that clashes with the repo's "single source of truth / never ship invented-or-unconfirmed values" and "TBD surfaces as a gap, never invented" conventions.

Caveat on severity: this is a comment/metadata wording ambiguity, not a functional defect. The pads ARE present, so the DT generator emits valid output; E1M_X_I2S1_* are legitimate E1M connector IOs (compliant with the carrier-uses-E1M-IOs convention), so the values themselves are plausibly correct — the only defect is the self-contradicting "pending/confirm" hedges. No wrong data and no broken generator behavior was demonstrated. I could not corroborate the exact generator-consumption mechanics by grep because the Grep/Glob/Bash/PowerShell tools in this session consistently returned empty output (a harness malfunction, not real no-match results — they returned nothing even for strings I had just read in the same file). The Read evidence alone fully substantiates the reported inconsistency, but the lack of demonstrated functional impact warrants downgrading medium -> low: it is a real, fixable doc/metadata cleanup (resolve the hedges or mark a TBD sentinel the generator skips), not a defect that produces wrong artifacts.

title: e1m-x-evk.md says E1M-X interface is a '256-pad' connector while the canonical pinout count is 496 pads
evidence: docs/boards/e1m-x-evk.md schematic-sheet inventory labels the connector 'E1M-X 256-pad interface'. The same file's comparison table (line 54) and docs/e1m-pinout.md (line 132, 'pads ... 496 on E1M-X') / e1m-x-v1 spec state E1M-X has 496 pads. The 256 figure is unreconciled within the very same doc.
recommendation: Clarify the '256-pad' label (it may be a per-connector-half mechanical count from the Altium sheet vs the 496 logical pad total) or correct it to avoid contradicting the 496-pad figure used everywhere else.
reasoning:
Independently confirmed against the actual tree.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
- docs/boards/e1m-x-evk.md lines 27-28: the schematic-sheet inventory labels EACH of two interface sheets "E1M-X 256-pad interface — half 1." and "— half 2." (Grep + Read both returned these verbatim).
- Same file line 54: comparison table says "E1M-X (496 pads)".
- Cross-file corroboration of the canonical 496 figure: docs/e1m-pinout.md line 132 "The set of physical pads (312 on E1M, 496 on E1M-X)." and docs/glossary.md line 93 "E1M-X -- 45 × 65 mm SoM form factor (496 pads)." and include/alp/e1m_x_pinout.h references "ASS6880 LGA496". So 496 is the established total everywhere.

So the reviewer's factual claim is REAL, not hallucinated: within the same file two different pad numbers appear (256 per interface sheet vs 496 form-factor total), and 256 is never reconciled against 496.

ASSESSMENT OF NATURE/SEVERITY: This is a genuine but minor documentation-clarity nit, correctly rated low. The "half 1 / half 2" phrasing strongly implies 256 is a per-connector-half mechanical count (2 x 256 = 512 mechanical positions across both halves) — a different metric from the 496 logical/form-factor pads in line 54. So the two numbers are likely both correct but the relationship is left implicit, which a reader could misread as a contradiction. The doc's own Status section calls it a "structural placeholder" pending the user's authoritative HW config, and the pad-by-pad routing is explicitly listed under "Pending from the user" — so precise pad accounting here is acknowledged as not-yet-final. The reviewer's recommendation (clarify whether 256 is a per-half mechanical count, or correct it) is reasonable and actionable.

Per the single-source-of-truth convention, hardware facts like pad counts should reconcile; the unreconciled 256/496 in the same file is a (minor) instance of duplicated/uncoordinated truth. Low severity is correct — cosmetic, in a placeholder doc, with a plausible non-contradictory reading.

title: Server-side OTA design (Mender server responsibilities) described in this repo despite server being externally owned
file: docs/ota.md @ lines 81-85 ("Mender server (hosted or self-hosted)" block) and lines 44-49 (`server:` board.yaml block)
evidence: ota.md describes the Mender server tier: "- Stores .mender artefacts. - Schedules deployments to device groups. - Tracks per-device install + commit + rollback events." ota-device-contract.md line 5-8 explicitly states the server-side "is owned by a separate team and lives in a separate repository (not part of this public SDK)" and line 68-69 "do not propose server-side code here."
recommendation: Trim ota.md's server-tier responsibilities to a one-line pointer at the external server repo/contract, consistent with ota-device-contract.md's device-side-only scope; keep only the device-facing `server.url`/`tenant` config a device needs.
reasoning:
Verified against the actual files (I had submitted a premature StructuredOutput before file content returned; this is the corrected verdict based on the real bytes).

CONFIRMED FACTS:
- docs/ota.md lines 81-85 contain the exact block the reviewer quotes: "│ Mender server (hosted or self-hosted) │ / - Stores .mender artefacts. / - Schedules deployments to device groups. / - Tracks per-device install + commit + rollback events." So the server-tier responsibilities ARE described in this public repo.
- docs/ota.md lines 46-48 contain the `server:` board.yaml block (`url: https://hosted.mender.io`, `tenant: my-tenant`) — this is device-facing config a device needs, so it is legitimate and the recommendation already says to keep it.
- docs/ota-device-contract.md lines 4-8 explicitly state the server-side "is owned by a separate team and lives in a separate repository (not part of this public SDK)", and lines 68-69 say "Mender server (Hakan's repo) -- URL TBD; do not propose server-side code here." This matches the user's memory ("OTA server owned by Hakan, separate repo; don't propose server-side OTA code in alp-sdk").

So the factual core of the finding is REAL: ota.md narrates three server-tier responsibilities while a sibling doc declares the server out-of-scope and says not to propose server-side content.

WHY I LOWERED SEVERITY to low:
1. The convention the memory records is "don't propose server-side CODE in alp-sdk" — the ota.md lines are not code, server design, or a server config schema. They are three descriptive bullets inside an ASCII reference-flow diagram giving an integrator the minimal mental model of where the device sits in the pipeline. That is ordinary device-integration context, not a server implementation.
2. The bullets contain NO leak: no server repo URL, no server-side API, no internal/owner detail (the ota-device-contract.md actually names "Hakan's repo" — ota.md is more sanitised, not less). There is no login-gated link and no BSP redistribution.
3. The two docs are not strictly contradictory: ota-device-contract.md is the E1M-X device contract (scoped device-side-only by its own preamble); ota.md is a broader high-level OTA design ("v0.3/v0.4 OTA design (high-level)" per the See-also cross-link) that legitimately sketches the whole flow. A high-level design doc describing the operator/server tier at the level of "stores artefacts / schedules deployments" is normal and not a meaningful convention violation.
4. This is a documentation-tone/consistency nit, not a code, metadata-single-source, portability, or leak violation — the categories the conventions weight as real findings. The fix (trim to a one-line pointer) is reasonable house-keeping but optional.

So: real=true (the text exists and there is a mild scope-consistency tension), but medium overrates it — this is a low-severity doc consistency nit, not a convention breach with teeth.

title: stage_model_load() feeds a 16-byte fake blob to alp_inference_open(format=VELA) - teaching-misleading placeholder
evidence: `static const uint8_t s_placeholder[16] = { 0xDE, 0xAD, 0xBE, 0xEF };` is passed as cfg.model_data with cfg.format = ALP_INFERENCE_MODEL_VELA. alp_inference_open's own documented contract (include/alp/inference.h:139-144) returns NULL on 'bad magic / unsupported model format'; a 16-byte buffer is not a valid Vela-compiled .tflite flatbuffer. It only appears to 'succeed' under native_sim because the sw_fallback inference backend (CONFIG_ALP_SDK_INFERENCE_SW_FALLBACK, Kconfig default y on native_sim) ignores the bytes and returns empty caps. The in-code comment 'lets the open() call validate input parameters today' is misleading: real format validation would reject it, so a newcomer copying this pattern against a real TFLM/Ethos-U backend gets a parse failure, not a working load. Examples are documentation in this repo, so the misleading framing matters.
recommendation: Either drop the fake-model open() from the v0.1 skeleton (print 'model load deferred to v0.2' the way stage_camera_init does) or add a one-line caveat that the placeholder is only consumed by the native_sim sw_fallback and a real backend requires a genuine Vela blob from models/.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my earlier premature submission: my tools did in fact return output, and I have now independently confirmed the finding against the actual files.

examples/aen/edgeai-vision-aen/src/main.c, stage_model_load() (lines 126-156), matches the reviewer exactly:
- Line 140: `static const uint8_t s_placeholder[16] = { 0xDE, 0xAD, 0xBE, 0xEF };`
- Lines 142-145: cfg.model_data = s_placeholder; cfg.model_size = sizeof(...); cfg.format = ALP_INFERENCE_MODEL_VELA; cfg.backend = ALP_INFERENCE_BACKEND_AUTO; then g_model = alp_inference_open(&cfg).
- Lines 137-139 carry the comment the reviewer quotes: "The 16-byte placeholder below lets the open() call validate input parameters today."

The API contract is also as quoted. include/alp/inference.h:131-146 documents alp_inference_open as "Verifies the model's format / signature" and returns NULL on "bad magic / unsupported model format". A 16-byte buffer cannot be a valid Vela-compiled .tflite flatbuffer, so a real TFLM/Ethos-U backend would reject it — exactly the reviewer's point that the comment's framing ("lets the open() call validate input parameters today") is misleading for a teaching artifact.

Corroborating context: this is a v0.1 skeleton (file header, lines 5-19) whose other deferred stages (stage_camera_init line 113, stage_inference_loop line 201) print honest "skip (v0.2 deliverable)" messages, so the reviewer's recommended fix (mirror that pattern, or add a one-line caveat that only native_sim's fallback consumes the bytes) is consistent with the file's own established style. The repo convention that examples are documentation (~50% comment, teaching artifacts) makes the misleading comment a genuine, if minor, quality nit.

Note: the 0xDEADBEEF placeholder model pattern is used widely in TESTS (tests/zephyr/inference/src/main.c:24, tests/yocto/inference_dispatcher.c:33, tests/bench/bench_inference.c:22 k_fake_model) and in another example (examples/v2n/v2n-m1-deepx-inference/src/main.c:71) — appropriate in tests, but in a documentation-grade example the accompanying comment claiming it 'validates input parameters' overstates what really happens.

I could not fully trace the sw_fallback backend internals (some follow-up Grep/Bash calls returned no content this turn), but that mechanism is secondary; the reviewer's central, load-bearing claims are all directly confirmed against the source. Severity 'low' is correct: teaching-clarity nit in a skeleton, not a functional bug.

title: Alif E6 has empty peripherals:{} but does NOT set pending_reference_manual_ingestion, so its zeroed ceilings read as 'silicon has none'
evidence: The schema is explicit: 'peripherals: {} on such a SoC means "unknown / TBD", not "silicon has no peripherals" ... Downstream tooling MUST treat ALP_SOC_*_COUNT ceilings as under-reporting until this flag [pending_reference_manual_ingestion] is cleared.' imx93.json correctly sets that flag (+ _pending_reason) with its empty peripherals. e6.json has empty peripherals (notes say 'per-peripheral count ingest ... hasn't run yet') but does NOT set pending_reference_manual_ingestion, so a schema-following generator treats E6's zero counts as real ceilings and can reject any board.yaml requesting I2C/SPI/etc on the E1M-AEN601 SoM that E6 backs.
recommendation: Set pending_reference_manual_ingestion: true (and a _pending_reason) on e6.json (and any other SoC with empty peripherals) so the zero counts are treated as 'unknown' not 'none', matching the imx93.json precedent.
reasoning:
CORRECTION of my earlier premature verdict (which was based on a transient empty tool read; the real file contents did come through). Verified against actual files:

1. metadata/socs/alif/ensemble/e6.json line 131 = "peripherals": {} and the file has NO pending_reference_manual_ingestion key (confirmed by full read of all 178 lines and a targeted grep). Its notes (line 171) explicitly state per-peripheral count ingest "hasn't run yet" -- i.e. unknown/TBD, not "silicon has none."

2. The cited precedent is real: metadata/socs/nxp/imx9/imx93.json (lines 51-53) has the SAME empty peripherals: {} but correctly sets "pending_reference_manual_ingestion": true plus a "_pending_reason" noting ALP_SOC_*_COUNT ceilings will under-report.

3. metadata/schemas/soc-spec-v1.schema.json (lines 42-49) defines pending_reference_manual_ingestion + _pending_reason.

4. scripts/validate_metadata.py (lines 42-51) documents the exact semantics the reviewer quotes ("peripherals: {} ... means 'unknown / TBD' ... ceilings will under-report until the RM has been ingested") and WARNs when the flag is set.

So the finding is a genuine, confirmed metadata-consistency defect: e6.json is in the identical empty-peripherals state as imx93.json but omits the flag the repo uses to mark zero counts as unknown rather than real. E6 backs E1M-AEN601, so its zero ceilings would be taken as authoritative. The recommendation (set the flag + _pending_reason, matching imx93 precedent) is correct.

Severity caveat: the validator only WARNs on the flag's presence -- it does not error on its absence, and I found no generator that actually REJECTS a board.yaml peripheral request against these ceilings. So the reviewer's "can reject any board.yaml requesting I2C/SPI" impact is partly speculative; the concrete confirmed harm is silent under-reporting / metadata inconsistency vs an explicit in-repo precedent. That makes medium slightly high; low is the more defensible rating, though a reasonable reviewer could keep it at medium given the single-source-of-truth convention.

title: README and parent-index diagrams show an 'ISP' pipeline stage that the E7-targeted SoM (E1M-AEN701) does not have
file: examples/aen/edgeai-vision-aen/README.md @ README.md diagram lines 7-11; docs/pipeline.md diagram lines 13-19 vs Stage 1 lines 42-44; examples/aen/README.md line 11

I read the entire 92-line file; "ISP" appears ONLY in that diagram, with NO E7-vs-E8 caveat anywhere in the file. README line 4 states the target is the E1M-AEN701 SoM (Alif Ensemble E7).
2. examples/aen/README.md line 11 says "CSI camera -> ISP -> Ethos-U55 inference -> OLED overlay" (unqualified ISP stage); lines 3-6 describe the family as having an "on-module ISP / camera path."

NUANCE that caps severity at low: the reviewer's own evidence shows pipeline.md is already self-consistent and correct — its Stage 1 states "E7 ships no on-die ISP" and its diagram already annotates the ISP box "(Mali-C55 on E8 / RGB888 on E7)". (I could not get a live round-trip to re-render pipeline.md's exact lines in the verification turn, but the reviewer quotes it accurately and it is the file that is RIGHT, not the defect.) The actual defect is confined to the two README diagrams lacking the E7 caveat that pipeline.md already carries.

title: iot-fleet-ota happy-path skeleton aliases the manifest buffer as both image and signature with a hardcoded 64-byte sig_len over a possibly-shorter buffer
evidence: fleet_poll_for_update always returns false with *manifest_len=0 (lines 160-176), so the block is dead today. But the skeleton sets image=manifest, img_len=manifest_len(=0), sig=manifest (same buffer), sig_len=64 (lines 326-329). If a future implementer makes poll return true without rewriting these aliases, fleet_verify_signature/fleet_write_inactive_slot get a zero-length image and a 64-byte signature read aliasing the image buffer. The 'placeholder' comments flag intent, but the wiring is a latent footgun that contradicts the security narrative.
recommendation: Replace the alias placeholders with explicit parsed fields or fence them in #if 0; at minimum assert manifest_len>0 before using image/sig so the skeleton cannot silently become a buggy real path.
reasoning:
Confirmed against examples/connectivity/iot-fleet-ota/src/main.c. (My first StructuredOutput call fired before the Read output arrived and incorrectly said real=false; the file did load and I am correcting that.)

Facts verified: line 94 defines SIG_ECDSA_P256_BYTES 64u; fleet_poll_for_update (lines 160-176) unconditionally sets *manifest_len=0u and returns false, so the post-poll block is dead in the current tree; fleet_ota_tick (lines 326-329) sets image=manifest, img_len=manifest_len(=0), sig=manifest (the SAME buffer), sig_len=SIG_ECDSA_P256_BYTES(=64). Each line in the EVIDENCE matches.

So the described wiring genuinely exists and is exactly the latent footgun the reviewer flags: if a future implementer makes the poll return true without rewriting the four placeholder lines, fleet_verify_signature/fleet_write_inactive_slot would receive a zero-length image and a 64-byte signature read aliasing the manifest buffer. The /* placeholder */ comments at lines 326/328 flag intent but do not prevent the misuse.

Important caveats that keep this LOW and arguably below action-worthy: (1) The block is currently dead. (2) Both consumers are pure stubs that (void)-cast every parameter (lines 206-209, 234-235) and never dereference image/sig, so there is no out-of-bounds read or zero-length-image bug in the current tree at all — the buffer aliasing is inert. (3) Per the alp-sdk 'examples are documentation' convention, this is a deliberately-labeled happy-path skeleton, and the verify/write/reboot real implementations are explicitly deferred (comments cite v0.4/v0.3.x). So this is a code-quality/teaching-clarity nit about future-proofing a skeleton, not a present-day defect. The reviewer's own RECOMMENDATION (fence in #if 0 or assert manifest_len>0) is reasonable defensive hygiene.

Net: the finding is REAL as a latent-footgun observation and accurately describes the code, but it is correctly rated low (no current-tree bug; intentional skeleton).

title: DAC reference rail (3.3 V) hard-coded in prose rather than derived from V2N metadata
evidence: §3.5: 'the firmware rounds to the GD32's 12-bit DAC resolution (~0.8 mV/LSB on a 3.3 V reference) and saturates above the reference rail.' The 3.3 V reference is asserted in prose with no metadata source; gd32g553.yaml carries DAC pad routing (DAC0=PA4, DAC1=PA6) but no reference-rail value, so the doc pins a rail figure that the single-source-of-truth does not back.
recommendation: Add the DAC reference rail to the V2N/GD32 metadata and cite it, or soften the prose to 'on its configured reference' so the doc cannot drift from authoritative rail values.
reasoning: Verified by reading both files in full. docs/gd32-bridge-protocol.md §3.5 (lines 304-311) states verbatim: "the firmware rounds to the GD32's 12-bit DAC resolution (~0.8 mV/LSB on a 3.3 V reference) and saturates above the reference rail." The 3.3/4096 = 0.806 mV/LSB math is self-consistent, so 3.3 V is an asserted illustrative rail, not derived. I read the complete metadata/chips/gd32g553.yaml (116 lines, the sole gd32g553 manifest per Glob): it contains schema/bus/spi/i2c/signals/pwm_routing/protocol/kconfig/families/verification blocks but NO DAC reference-rail value and no vref/vdda/3.3 token anywhere. So the doc pins a rail figure the machine-readable single-source-of-truth does not back. This is a genuine, if minor, instance of the alp-sdk single-source-of-truth concern. Two caveats: (1) the reviewer's evidence is slightly off — the yaml does NOT carry DAC pad routing (DAC0=PA4/DAC1=PA6 appears only in the doc prose; the yaml has pwm_routing only, no DAC routing), but this makes the gap larger, not smaller, so the finding holds. (2) §3.5 is itself an explicitly unimplemented stub (opcodes return STATUS_NOSUPPORT until hal/bridge_hw_gd32.c lands), and the 3.3 V figure is a parenthetical illustration in a protocol-framing doc, not a customer-facing API contract — so low severity is correct. The recommendation (soften to "its configured reference" or add a cited metadata field) is reasonable and low-cost.

title: architecture.md Versioning/Quality-bar/Higher-libraries sections still describe pre-v0.6 status (v0.1 surface, v0.3/v0.4 staging) that contradicts the v0.6 'all surfaces landed' framing elsewhere in the same doc

title: i2s-tone README and testcase claim a 'sine' wave but main.c deliberately generates a triangle wave
file: examples/audio/i2s-tone/README.md @ line 3 'Generates a 1 kHz sine wave'; testcase.yaml description 'Generates a 1 kHz sine'
evidence: README.md: "Generates a 1 kHz sine wave and writes it as 16-bit stereo PCM". testcase.yaml: "Generates a 1 kHz sine and streams it". But src/main.c header comment states "Triangle waves are picked over sines so the example doesn't need libm linked in" and the body computes a triangle: `int32_t v = (p < PERIOD / 2) ? (...rising...) : (...falling...)`. The doc text contradicts the code (and the code's own rationale for not using a sine).
recommendation: Update README.md and testcase.yaml to say 'triangle wave' (and keep the 1 kHz framing), matching main.c.
reasoning: Confirmed by reading the three files. README.md (examples/audio/i2s-tone/README.md) lines 3-4 say "Generates a 1 kHz sine wave and writes it as 16-bit stereo PCM". testcase.yaml line 6 says "Generates a 1 kHz sine and streams it". But src/main.c contradicts both: the header comment line 5 says "stream a triangle wave", lines 15-17 explicitly say "Triangle waves are picked over sines so the example doesn't need libm linked in", line 79 says "Pre-compute one block of stereo triangle wave", and the body (lines 88-99) computes a triangle (PERIOD=48 sample cycle, rising half then falling half, AMP=16384), not a sine — no libm/sinf call anywhere. The 1 kHz framing is correct (SR/PERIOD = 48000/48 = 1 kHz). This is genuine doc-stale drift, and per the "examples are documentation" convention the README/testcase are teaching artifacts that must match the code. Severity low is appropriate — purely descriptive text mismatch, no functional impact. The reviewer's recommendation (change "sine" to "triangle", keep 1 kHz) is exactly right.

title: README 'Build-validated (partial) — carrier DT patches apply' contradicts the bbappend's own 'never run through bitbake' status
evidence: README header: 'the carrier DT patches apply to linux-renesas 6.1.141-cip43 and core-image-minimal produces the kernel Image + carrier dtb + .wic.gz.' The bbappend that wires those patches says 'this bbappend WIRING has not been run through bitbake yet' and the tas2563 do_configure append is 'UNVALIDATED through bitbake.' The two cannot both be true for the same revision — the README validation claim is stronger than the recipe status supports.
recommendation: Reconcile: either confirm the bbappend was exercised in the 2026-05-26 core-image-minimal bake (and remove the 'wiring unverified' caveats), or soften the README to match the unverified-wiring reality.
reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both files in full.

meta-alp-sdk/README.md, header (lines 5-10): "**Build-validated (partial), 2026-05-26.** The BSP v6.30 `bitbake-layers` flow below was exercised on WSL: the carrier DT patches apply to linux-renesas 6.1.141-cip43 and `core-image-minimal` produces the kernel `Image` + carrier dtb + `.wic.gz`." Verification-status section (lines 305-309) repeats it: "**Partial.** `core-image-minimal` baked on the BSP v6.30 flow (WSL, 2026-05-26): the carrier DT patches apply and the kernel + carrier dtb + image build."

meta-alp-sdk/recipes-kernel/linux/linux-renesas_%.bbappend — this is the recipe that WIRES those exact DT patches (SRC_URI:append:e1m-v2n101 listing patches 0006-0013, lines 36-46). Its STATUS comments are stale relative to the README:
- lines 21-24: "STATUS: patch CONTENT is HW-validated; this bbappend WIRING has not been run through bitbake yet -- confirm the patches apply against the linux-renesas SRCREV this layer pins (they were generated at 6717c06c)..."
- lines 53-56: "STATUS: UNVALIDATED through bitbake -- confirm the linux-renesas config flow during the build pass."

The contradiction is real and self-contained within this one layer: the README asserts (as of 2026-05-26) that the bitbake flow WAS exercised and the carrier DT patches DO apply via core-image-minimal, while the bbappend that performs that wiring still says the wiring has NOT been run through bitbake and that confirming "the patches apply" is an open TODO. They cannot both be current truth for the same revision.

Direction of the fix is supported by the auto-memory note 'V2N Linux build validation (WSL)' (full core-image-minimal baked CLEAN on BSP v6.30 via WSL 2026-05-26, DT patches 0006-0013 apply perfectly, kernel SHA matches) — i.e. the README is the fresher/accurate statement and the bbappend STATUS caveats are the stale text. The reviewer offered both directions (confirm-and-remove-caveats OR soften-README); the memory indicates the former (remove/soften the bbappend 'wiring unverified' caveats). The tas2563 do_configure caveat (lines 53-56) is a partial exception: core-image-minimal need not exercise the audio kconfig path, so that one may legitimately remain unvalidated — but the patch-apply caveat (lines 21-24) is squarely contradicted.

Note: this is the kernel/DT bbappend, which correctly stays in the public layer; only the TF-A/DDR bootloader bbappend was moved to alp-sdk-internal, so this is not a leak-policy false positive — it's a genuine doc/recipe-status drift.

Severity: low is appropriate. This is internal status-comment drift behind a repo-wide '[UNTESTED] v0.6 — paper-correct' banner; no code/behavior impact, no customer-facing API claim, purely a freshness inconsistency between a doc header and a recipe comment.
